   543	
   544	/// Checks if `argument` benefits from grouping in call arguments.
   545	fn can_group_expression_argument(argument: &Expression<'_>, f: &Formatter<'_, '_>) -> bool {
   546	    match argument {
   547	        Expression::ObjectExpression(object_expression) => {
   548	            !object_expression.properties.is_empty()
   549	                || f.comments().has_comment_in_span(object_expression.span)
   550	        }
   551	        Expression::ArrayExpression(array_expression) => {
   552	            !array_expression.elements.is_empty()
   553	                || f.comments().has_comment_in_span(array_expression.span)
   554	        }
   555	        Expression::TSTypeAssertion(assertion_expression) => {
   556	            can_group_expression_argument(&assertion_expression.expression, f)
   557	        }
   558	        Expression::TSAsExpression(as_expression) => {
   559	            can_group_expression_argument(&as_expression.expression, f)
   560	        }
   561	        Expression::TSSatisfiesExpression(satisfies_expression) => {
   562	            can_group_expression_argument(&satisfies_expression.expression, f)
   563	        }
   564	        Expression::ArrowFunctionExpression(arrow_function) => {
   565	            can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument(arrow_function, false, f)
   566	        }
   567	        Expression::FunctionExpression(_) => true,
   568	        _ => false,
   569	    }
   570	}
   571	
   572	fn can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument(
   573	    arrow_function: &ArrowFunctionExpression,
   574	    is_arrow_recursion: bool,
   575	    f: &Formatter<'_, '_>,
   576	) -> bool {
   577	    let body = &arrow_function.body;
   578	    let return_type_annotation = &arrow_function.return_type;
   579	
   580	    // Handles cases like:
   581	    //
   582	    // app.get("/", (req, res): void => {
   583	    //     res.send("Hello World!");
   584	    // });
   585	    //
   586	    // export class Thing implements OtherThing {
   587	    //   do: (type: Type) => Provider<Prop> = memoize(
   588	    //     (type: ObjectType): Provider<Opts> => {}
   589	    //   );
   590	    // }
   591	    let can_group_type = return_type_annotation.as_ref().is_none_or(|any_type| {
   592	        match &any_type.type_annotation {
   593	            TSType::TSTypeReference(_) => {
   594	                if arrow_function.expression {
   595	                    return false;
   596	                }
   597	                body.statements.iter().any(|statement| match statement {
   598	                    #[expect(clippy::match_same_arms)]
   599	                    Statement::EmptyStatement(_) => {
   600	                        // When the body contains an empty statement, comments in
   601	                        // the body will get attached to that statement rather than
   602	                        // the body itself, so they need to be checked for comments
   603	                        // as well to ensure that the body is still considered
   604	                        // groupable when those empty statements are removed by the
   605	                        // printer.
   606	                        // TODO: it seems no difference if we comment out this line
   607	                        // comments.has_comments(s.span)
   608	                        true
   609	                    }
   610	                    _ => true,
   611	                }) || (body.statements.is_empty() && f.comments().has_comment_before(body.span.end))
   612	            }
   613	            _ => true,
   614	        }
   615	    });
   616	
   617	    if !can_group_type {
   618	        return false;
   619	    }
   620

   636	
   637	fn write_grouped_arguments<'a>(
   638	    node: &AstNode<'a, ArenaVec<'a, Argument<'a>>>,
   639	    group_layout: GroupedCallArgumentLayout,
   640	    f: &mut Formatter<'_, 'a>,
   641	) {
   642	    let last_index = node.len() - 1;
   643	    let only_one_argument = last_index == 0;
   644	    let mut non_grouped_breaks = false;
   645	    let mut grouped_breaks = false;
   646	    let mut has_cached = false;
   647	
   648	    // Pre-format the arguments to determine if they can be grouped.
   649	    let elements = node
   650	        .iter()
   651	        .enumerate()
   652	        .map(|(index, argument)| {
   653	            let is_grouped_argument = (group_layout.is_grouped_first() && index == 0)
   654	                || (group_layout.is_grouped_last() && index == last_index);
   655	
   656	            // We have to get the lines before the argument has been formatted, because it relies on
   657	            // the comments before the argument. After formatting, the comments might marked as printed,
   658	            // which would lead to a wrong line count.
   659	            let lines_before = f.source_text().get_lines_before(argument.span(), f.comments());
   660	            let comma = (last_index != index).then_some(",");
   661	
   662	            let interned = f.intern(&format_once(|f| {
   663	                if is_grouped_argument {
   664	                    match argument.as_ast_nodes() {
   665	                        AstNodes::Function(function)
   666	                            if !group_layout.is_grouped_first()
   667	                                && (!only_one_argument
   668	                                    || function_has_only_simple_parameters(&function.params)) =>
   669	                        {
   670	                            has_cached = true;
   671	                            return write!(
   672	                                f,
   673	                                [
   674	                                    FormatFunction::new_with_options(
   675	                                        function,
   676	                                        FormatFunctionOptions {
   677	                                            cache_mode: FunctionCacheMode::Cache,
   678	                                            ..FormatFunctionOptions::default()
   679	                                        },
   680	                                    ),
   681	                                    comma
   682	                                ]
   683	                            );
   684	                        }
   685	                        AstNodes::ArrowFunctionExpression(arrow) => {
   686	                            has_cached = true;
   687	                            return write!(
   688	                                f,
   689	                                [
   690	                                    FormatJsArrowFunctionExpression::new_with_options(
   691	                                        arrow,
   692	                                        FormatJsArrowFunctionExpressionOptions {
   693	                                            cache_mode: FunctionCacheMode::Cache,
   694	                                            ..FormatJsArrowFunctionExpressionOptions::default()
   695	                                        },
   696	                                    ),
   697	                                    comma
   698	                                ]
   699	                            );
   700	                        }
   701	                        _ => {}
   702	                    }
   703	                }
   704	                write!(f, [argument, comma]);
   705	            }));
   706	
   707	            let break_type =
   708	                if is_grouped_argument { &mut grouped_breaks } else { &mut non_grouped_breaks };
   709	
   710	            *break_type = *break_type || interned.as_ref().is_some_and(FormatElement::will_break);
   711	
   712	            (interned, lines_before)
   713	        })
   714	        .collect::<Vec<_>>();
   715	
   716	    // If any of the not grouped elements break, then fall back to the variant where
   717	    // all arguments are printed in expanded mode.
   718	    if non_grouped_breaks {
   719	        return format_all_elements_broken_out(node, elements.into_iter(), true, f);
   720	    }
   721	
   722	    // First write the most expanded variant because it needs `arguments`.
   723	    let most_expanded = {
   724	        let mut buffer = VecBuffer::new(f.state_mut());
   725	        buffer.write_element(FormatElement::Tag(Tag::StartEntry));
   726	
   727	        format_all_elements_broken_out(node, elements.iter().cloned(), true, &mut buffer);
   728	
   729	        buffer.write_element(FormatElement::Tag(Tag::EndEntry));
   730	
   731	        buffer.into_vec().into_bump_slice()
   732	    };
   733	
   734	    // Now reformat the first or last argument if they happen to be a function or arrow function expression.
   735	    // Function and arrow function expression apply a custom formatting that removes soft line breaks from the parameters,
   736	    // type parameters, and return type annotation.
   737	    //
   738	    // This implementation caches the function body of the "normal" formatted function or arrow function expression
   739	    // to avoid quadratic complexity if the functions' body contains another call expression with an arrow or function expression
   740	    // as first or last argument.
   741	    let mut grouped = elements;
   742	    if has_cached {
   743	        let (argument, grouped_element) = match group_layout {
   744	            GroupedCallArgumentLayout::GroupedFirstArgument => {
   745	                (node.first().unwrap(), &mut grouped.first_mut().unwrap().0)
   746	            }
   747	            GroupedCallArgumentLayout::GroupedLastArgument => {
   748	                (node.last().unwrap(), &mut grouped.last_mut().unwrap().0)
   749	            }
   750	        };

2026-05-31 11:45:39,595 - INFO - swea-agent-oxc-project__oxc-20857 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now I see the issue better. The `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` function determines if an arrow function can be "grouped" as a call argument. If it can be grouped, it's treated specially (tries to keep it on one line).

Looking at line 621:
```rust
arrow_function.get_expression().is_none_or(|expr| match expr {
    Expression::ObjectExpression(_)
    | Expression::ArrayExpression(_)
    | Expression::JSXElement(_)
    | Expression::JSXFragment(_) => true,
    Expression::ArrowFunctionExpression(inner_arrow_function) => {
        can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument(inner_arrow_function, true, f)
    }
    Expression::ChainExpression(chain) => {
        matches!(chain.expression, ChainElement::CallExpression(_)) && !is_arrow_recursion
    }
    Expression::CallExpression(_) | Expression::ConditionalExpression(_) => !is_arrow_recursion,
    _ => false,
})
```

The body expression of the arrow is `visit(child)` which is a `CallExpression`. So this matches `Expression::CallExpression(_) => !is_arrow_recursion`, which returns `true` (since `is_arrow_recursion` is `false` for the first call). So the arrow function IS groupable.

Now, when an arrow function is grouped, it's formatted with `FormatJsArrowFunctionExpression` (line 685-699) without the `GroupedCallArgumentLayout` being set! Notice that the `FormatJsArrowFunctionExpressionOptions` doesn't include the `call_argument_layout`:

```rust
FormatJsArrowFunctionExpressionOptions {
    cache_mode: FunctionCacheMode::Cache,
    ..FormatJsArrowFunctionExpressionOptions::default()
},
```

The default for `call_argument_layout` is `None`. But in the `format` method of `FormatJsArrowFunctionExpression`, there's logic at lines 261-263:

```rust
let is_non_grouped_or_grouped_last_argument = matches!(
    options.call_argument_layout,
    None | Some(GroupedCallArgumentLayout::GroupedLastArgument)
);
```

This is used for determining arrow chain layout. But the `call_argument_layout` is also used later at line 188-191:

```rust
let is_last_call_arg = matches!(
    self.options.call_argument_layout,
    Some(GroupedCallArgumentLayout::GroupedLastArgument)
);
```

And `should_add_soft_line` depends on it.

But wait, the problem is different. The arrow function IS being grouped. When it's grouped, it should try to stay on one line. But the body has a leading JSDoc comment `/** @type {SomeType} */`. Let me check how this comment affects formatting.

Let me look at `FormatNodeWithoutTrailingComments` since the arrow body is formatted through `FormatMaybeCachedFunctionBody` which calls `expression.fmt(f)`. When formatting a `CallExpression`, it first formats leading comments. The comment `/** @type {SomeType} */` is a block comment attached to the `CallExpression` (or more precisely, to the `ParenthesizedExpression` that was removed, so it becomes attached to the inner expression).

Let me check where the leading comment formatting might cause a break. The key is in `format_leading_comments` in trivia.rs:

    89	/// Formats the leading comments of `node`
    90	#[inline]
    91	pub const fn format_leading_comments<'a>(span: Span) -> FormatLeadingComments<'a> {
    92	    FormatLeadingComments::Node(span)
    93	}
    94	
    95	/// Formats the leading comments of a node.
    96	#[derive(Debug, Copy, Clone)]
    97	pub enum FormatLeadingComments<'a> {
    98	    Node(Span),
    99	    Comments(&'a [Comment]),
   100	}
   101	
   102	impl<'a> Format<'a> for FormatLeadingComments<'a> {
   103	    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut Formatter<'_, 'a>) {
   104	        fn format_leading_comments_impl<'a>(
   105	            comments: impl IntoIterator<Item = &'a Comment>,
   106	            f: &mut Formatter<'_, 'a>,
   107	        ) {
   108	            let mut leading_comments_iter = comments.into_iter().peekable();
   109	            while let Some(comment) = leading_comments_iter.next() {
   110	                f.context_mut().comments_mut().increment_printed_count();
   111	                write!(f, comment);
   112	
   113	                match comment.kind {
   114	                    CommentKind::SingleLineBlock | CommentKind::MultiLineBlock => {
   115	                        match f.source_text().lines_after(comment.span.end) {
   116	                            0 => {
   117	                                let should_nestle =
   118	                                    leading_comments_iter.peek().is_some_and(|next_comment| {
   119	                                        should_nestle_adjacent_doc_comments(comment, next_comment)
   120	                                    });
   121	
   122	                                write!(f, [maybe_space(!should_nestle)]);
   123	                            }
   124	                            1 => {
   125	                                if f.source_text().get_lines_before(comment.span, f.comments()) == 0
   126	                                {
   127	                                    write!(f, [soft_line_break_or_space()]);
   128	                                } else {
   129	                                    write!(f, [hard_line_break()]);
   130	                                }
   131	                            }
   132	                            _ => write!(f, [empty_line()]),
   133	                        }
   134	                    }
   135	                    CommentKind::Line => match f.source_text().lines_after(comment.span.end) {
   136	                        0 | 1 => write!(f, [hard_line_break()]),
   137	                        _ => write!(f, [empty_line()]),
   138	                    },
   139	                }
   140	            }
   141	        }
   142	
   143	        match self {
   144	            Self::Node(span) => {
   145	                let leading_comments = f.context().comments().comments_before(span.start);
   146	                if leading_comments.is_empty() {
   147	                    return;
   148	                }
   149	                format_leading_comments_impl(leading_comments, f);
   150	            }
   151	            Self::Comments(comments) => {
   152	                if comments.is_empty() {
   153	                    return;
   154	                }
   155	                format_leading_comments_impl(*comments, f);
   156	            }
   157	        }
   158	    }
   159	}

   166	    following_span_start: u32,
   167	) -> FormatTrailingComments<'a> {
   168	    FormatTrailingComments::Node((enclosing_span, preceding_span, following_span_start))
   169	}

   708	fn should_add_parens(body: &AstNode<'_, FunctionBody<'_>>) -> bool {
   709	    let AstNodes::ExpressionStatement(stmt) = body.statements().first().unwrap().as_ast_nodes()
   710	    else {
   711	        unreachable!()
   712	    };
   713	
   714	    // Add parentheses to avoid confusion between `a => b ? c : d` and `a <= b ? c : d`
   715	    // but only if the body isn't an object/function or class expression because parentheses are always required in that
   716	    // case and added by the object expression itself
   717	    if matches!(&stmt.expression, Expression::ConditionalExpression(_)) {
   718	        !matches!(
   719	            ExpressionLeftSide::leftmost(stmt.expression()).as_ref(),
   720	            Expression::ObjectExpression(_)
   721	                | Expression::FunctionExpression(_)
   722	                | Expression::ClassExpression(_)
   723	        )
   724	    } else {
   725	        false
   726	    }
   727	}
   728	
   729	fn has_rest_object_or_array_parameter(params: &FormalParameters) -> bool {
   730	    params.rest.is_some()
   731	        || params.items.iter().any(|param| param.pattern.is_destructuring_pattern())
   732	}
   733	
   734	/// Writes the arrow function type parameters, parameters, and return type annotation.
   735	///
   736	/// Formats the parameters and return type annotation without any soft line breaks if `is_grouped_call_argument` is `true`
   737	/// so that the parameters and return type are kept on the same line.
   738	fn format_signature<'a, 'b>(
   739	    arrow: &'b AstNode<'a, ArrowFunctionExpression<'a>>,
   740	    is_grouped_call_argument: bool,
   741	    is_first_in_chain: bool,
   742	    cache_mode: FunctionCacheMode,
   743	) -> impl Format<'a> + 'b {
   744	    format_with(move |f| {
   745	        let content = format_with(|f| {
   746	            group(&format_args!(
   747	                arrow.r#async().then_some("async "),
   748	                arrow.type_parameters(),
   749	                arrow.params(),
   750	                arrow.return_type().map(FormatNodeWithoutTrailingComments),
   751	            ))
   752	            .fmt(f);
   753	        });
   754	        let format_head = FormatContentWithCacheMode::new(arrow.params.span, content, cache_mode);
   755	
   756	        if is_grouped_call_argument {
   757	            // The first arrow's soft lines have already been removed in the CallArguments.
   758	            if is_first_in_chain {
   759	                write!(f, format_head);
   760	            } else {
   761	                write!(f, [space()]);
   762	                let mut buffer = RemoveSoftLinesBuffer::new(f);
   763	                write!(buffer, format_head);
   764	            }
   765	        } else {
   766	            write!(
   767	                f,
   768	                [
   769	                    // This soft break is placed outside of the group to ensure
   770	                    // that the parameter group only tries to write on a single
   771	                    // line and can't break pre-emptively without also causing
   772	                    // the parent (i.e., this ArrowChain) to break first.
   773	                    (!is_first_in_chain).then_some(soft_line_break_or_space()),
   774	                    format_head
   775	                ]
   776	            );
   777	        }
   778	
   779	        // Print comments before the fat arrow (`=>`)
   780	        let comments_before_fat_arrow =
   781	            f.context().comments().comments_before_character(arrow.params.span().end, b'=');
   782	        let content = FormatTrailingComments::Comments(comments_before_fat_arrow);
   783	        write!(f, [FormatContentWithCacheMode::new(arrow.span, content, cache_mode)]);
   784	    })
   785	}

2026-05-31 11:46:00,489 - INFO - swea-agent-oxc-project__oxc-20857 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now let me think about this differently. The PR issue is about the decision to break after `=>` vs breaking the call arguments. Let me look at how the arrow function body with a leading comment influences the formatting.

In the arrow function `(child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))`, the body is an expression `visit(child)` with a leading comment `/** @type {SomeType} */`. 

When we go through the `else` branch (since `body_has_soft_line_break` is false), we use `soft_line_indent_or_space`. The content includes formatting the body (which first formats leading comments, then the expression). 

The key issue might be that the leading comment `/** @type {SomeType} */` on the body expression causes the group to expand (break). Let me check if `soft_line_indent_or_space` causes a break when the content contains a comment...

Actually, wait. Let me re-read the PR description more carefully. The input already has the call broken across multiple lines:

```js
const result = items.map(
  (child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))
);
```

The formatter should preserve this layout (break after the opening paren) and keep the arrow function on one line. But Oxfmt is collapsing it to:

```js
const result = items.map((child) =>
  /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))
);
```

So Oxfmt is:
1. Collapsing the call arguments to a single line
2. Then breaking after `=>` because the body doesn't fit

The expected behavior (Prettier) is:
1. Keep the call arguments expanded (broken after opening paren)
2. Keep the arrow function on one line

So the issue is about how the call arguments layout is determined. The arrow function is grouped as the last argument, and when it's grouped, the formatter tries to put everything on one line. But the arrow function body with the JSDoc comment is too long, so it breaks after `=>`.

The fix should be: when an arrow function body has a leading JSDoc block comment (like `/** @type {X} */`), the arrow function should NOT be grouped, or should be treated differently so that the call arguments break first.

Let me look at how Prettier handles this. In Prettier, there's a concept of "shouldGroupLast" and "shouldGroupFirst". Looking at the Prettier source for `canGroupArrowFunctionExpressionArgument`, the key check is whether the arrow body is "simple" enough to be grouped.

Let me search for any existing handling of leading comments in the arrow function grouping logic:

Now let me look at the `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` function more carefully. The issue is that when the arrow body is a `CallExpression` (like `visit(child)`), it returns `true` (groupable). But when there's a leading JSDoc comment, the arrow function shouldn't be grouped, or the grouping should behave differently.

Let me check the Prettier source for comparison. Let me look at how Prettier handles this specific case.

Actually, let me look at the `can_group_expression_argument` function and `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` more carefully. The issue might be that when we have a leading comment on the body, we should not group the arrow function.

Let me check what happens when the body has a leading comment:

In `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument`:
1. It checks the return type annotation (none in this case)
2. It checks the body expression: `arrow_function.get_expression().is_none_or(|expr| ...)` 

The body expression is a `CallExpression` (`visit(child)`), so it returns `true` (groupable).

But maybe we need to also check if the body expression has leading comments that would make the arrow function not groupable? Let me check if there's a check for leading comments in this function.

Looking at the function, there's no check for leading comments on the body expression. But there's a check in `should_group_last_argument_impl` at lines 390-402:

```rust
let has_comment_before_last = if let Some(penultimate) = penultimate {
    // Check for comments between the penultimate and last argument
    f.comments().comments_in_range(penultimate.span().end, last_span.start).last().is_some_and(
        |c| {
            // Exclude end-of-line comments (treated as previous node's comment)
            // and comments followed by a comma
            !c.followed_by_newline()
                && !f.source_text().next_non_whitespace_byte_is(c.span.end, b',')
        },
    )
} else {
    f.comments().has_comment_before(last_span.start)
};

if has_comment_before_last
    // Check for comments after the last argument
    || f.comments().comments_after(last_span.end).first().is_some_and(|c|
    !f.source_text().bytes_contain(last_span.end, c.span.start, b')'))
{
    return false;
}
```

This checks for comments between the penultimate and last argument, but not for comments inside the last argument (like leading comments on the arrow body).

The issue might be that we need to check if the arrow function body has a leading comment that makes it "not simple" enough to group. Let me look at how Prettier handles this.

2026-05-31 11:46:23,594 - INFO - swea-agent-oxc-project__oxc-20857 - 💭 THOUGHT
Let me think about this more carefully. The PR description says:

> When an arrow function body starts with a JSDoc inline type cast (`/** @type {X} */`), Prettier breaks after the opening parenthesis and keeps the entire arrow expression on one line, while Oxfmt breaks after `=>` and puts the JSDoc type cast + body on the next line.

So the key insight is: the JSDoc comment `/** @type {X} */` before the body expression should make the arrow function "not groupable" as a call argument. When it's not grouped, the call arguments will be formatted normally (with `soft_block_indent`), which allows them to break after the opening paren.

But wait, looking at the current behavior for the first example with printWidth 100:
```
const result = items.map((child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child)));
```

It stays on one line because it fits. That's correct for printWidth 100.

For the second example:
```
const body = transform_body(state.analysis.instance_body, b.id("$.run"), (node) =>
  /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node)),
);
```

It breaks after `=>`. The issue is that the arrow function is grouped, and the group tries to fit everything on one line, but it doesn't fit, so it breaks after `=>`.

But what would Prettier do? According to the PR, Prettier would break the call arguments:
```
const body = transform_body(
	state.analysis.instance_body,
	b.id('$.run'),
	(node) => /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node))
);
```

So the fix should be: when an arrow function has a leading JSDoc comment on its body, it should not be grouped as a call argument. Or more generally, the arrow function should not be grouped if its body has leading comments that would force a break.

Let me look at how Prettier implements this. Let me search for relevant Prettier code patterns:

Actually, let me check if there's a specific check in `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` that should check for leading comments on the body:

   572	fn can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument(
   573	    arrow_function: &ArrowFunctionExpression,
   574	    is_arrow_recursion: bool,
   575	    f: &Formatter<'_, '_>,
   576	) -> bool {
   577	    let body = &arrow_function.body;
   578	    let return_type_annotation = &arrow_function.return_type;
   579	
   580	    // Handles cases like:
   581	    //
   582	    // app.get("/", (req, res): void => {
   583	    //     res.send("Hello World!");
   584	    // });
   585	    //
   586	    // export class Thing implements OtherThing {
   587	    //   do: (type: Type) => Provider<Prop> = memoize(
   588	    //     (type: ObjectType): Provider<Opts> => {}
   589	    //   );
   590	    // }
   591	    let can_group_type = return_type_annotation.as_ref().is_none_or(|any_type| {
   592	        match &any_type.type_annotation {
   593	            TSType::TSTypeReference(_) => {
   594	                if arrow_function.expression {
   595	                    return false;
   596	                }
   597	                body.statements.iter().any(|statement| match statement {
   598	                    #[expect(clippy::match_same_arms)]
   599	                    Statement::EmptyStatement(_) => {
   600	                        // When the body contains an empty statement, comments in
   601	                        // the body will get attached to that statement rather than
   602	                        // the body itself, so they need to be checked for comments
   603	                        // as well to ensure that the body is still considered
   604	                        // groupable when those empty statements are removed by the
   605	                        // printer.
   606	                        // TODO: it seems no difference if we comment out this line
   607	                        // comments.has_comments(s.span)
   608	                        true
   609	                    }
   610	                    _ => true,
   611	                }) || (body.statements.is_empty() && f.comments().has_comment_before(body.span.end))
   612	            }
   613	            _ => true,
   614	        }
   615	    });
   616	
   617	    if !can_group_type {
   618	        return false;
   619	    }
   620	
   621	    arrow_function.get_expression().is_none_or(|expr| match expr {
   622	        Expression::ObjectExpression(_)
   623	        | Expression::ArrayExpression(_)
   624	        | Expression::JSXElement(_)
   625	        | Expression::JSXFragment(_) => true,
   626	        Expression::ArrowFunctionExpression(inner_arrow_function) => {
   627	            can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument(inner_arrow_function, true, f)
   628	        }
   629	        Expression::ChainExpression(chain) => {
   630	            matches!(chain.expression, ChainElement::CallExpression(_)) && !is_arrow_recursion
   631	        }
   632	        Expression::CallExpression(_) | Expression::ConditionalExpression(_) => !is_arrow_recursion,
   633	        _ => false,
   634	    })
   635	}
   636	




2026-05-31 11:46:24,250 - INFO - swea-agent-oxc-project__oxc-20857 - ========================= STEP 60 =========================
2026-05-31 11:46:24,258 - DEBUG - swea-lm-oxc-project__oxc-20857 - n_cache_control: 2
2026-05-31 11:47:01,875 - DEBUG - swea-lm-oxc-project__oxc-20857 - Response: ModelResponse(id='f04c9517f5734304be8aa4e04a799a04', created=1780227987, model='DeepSeek-V4-Pro', object='chat.completion', system_fingerprint=None, choices=[Choices(finish_reason='tool_calls', index=0, message=Message(content='Now I need to understand: when the arrow function body is a `CallExpression` with a leading comment, should it be groupable?\n\nThe answer from the PR description is: NO. When there\'s a JSDoc inline type cast comment before the body, the arrow function should not be grouped.\n\nBut how do we detect this? The comment `/** @type {SomeType} */` is a leading comment on the body expression. We need to check if the body expression has leading comments that would cause issues when grouping.\n\nActually, let me think about this differently. Maybe the fix should be in the `body_has_soft_line_break` logic. Currently, when the body is a `CallExpression` (or any expression not explicitly listed), `body_has_soft_line_break` is false, which leads to the `soft_line_indent_or_space` path. But maybe when there\'s a leading comment on the body expression, we should treat it like objects/arrays and use `body_has_soft_line_break = true` (which uses just `space()` then body, without the soft indent).\n\nWait, let me re-examine. When `body_has_soft_line_break` is true:\n```rust\nwrite!(f, [space(), format_body]);\n```\nThis puts a space then the body, without any group. The body is just formatted inline. This means the arrow function body stays on the same line as `=>` if it fits.\n\nWhen `body_has_soft_line_break` is false:\n```rust\ngroup(&format_args!(\n    soft_line_indent_or_space(&format_with(|f| {\n        // body content\n    })),\n    ...\n))\n```\nThis creates a group that either puts a space (if it fits) or breaks and indents.\n\nThe issue is that when the arrow is grouped as a call argument, the `soft_line_indent_or_space` break causes the arrow to break after `=>`. But if `body_has_soft_line_break` were true, it would just use `space()` and the arrow function would stay on one line, and if it doesn\'t fit in the call, the call arguments would break instead.\n\nSo the fix might be: when the arrow function body expression has a leading comment (specifically a block comment like JSDoc), we should set `body_has_soft_line_break` to true, so the arrow function stays on one line.\n\nBut wait, that would change behavior for all arrow functions with leading comments, not just JSDoc casts. Let me check what Prettier does with other types of leading comments.\n\nActually, let me look more at the `body_has_soft_line_break` logic. For objects, arrays, and arrow functions, it checks `!f.comments().has_leading_own_line_comment(body.span().start)`. If there\'s a leading own-line comment, `body_has_soft_line_break` becomes false (because of the `!`). \n\nWait, let me re-read:\n\n```rust\nExpression::ArrowFunctionExpression(_)\n| Expression::ArrayExpression(_)\n| Expression::ObjectExpression(_) => {\n    !f.comments().has_leading_own_line_comment(body.span().start)\n}\n```\n\nIf there\'s NO leading own-line comment, this returns `true` (body has soft line break). If there IS a leading own-line comment, it returns `false`.\n\nFor other expressions (like `CallExpression`):\n```rust\n_ => {\n    is_multiline_template_starting_on_same_line(expression, f.source_text())\n        || is_huggable_html_embed(expression, f)\n}\n```\nThis returns false unless it\'s a template or HTML embed.\n\nSo the issue is: `CallExpression` with a leading comment returns `body_has_soft_line_break = false`, which uses `soft_line_indent_or_space`. This causes the break after `=>`.\n\nBut what should happen? Let me check what Prettier does by looking at the PR description again:\n\nPrettier keeps the arrow function on one line. So for Prettier, the arrow function body with a JSDoc comment should NOT break after `=>`. It should stay on the same line.\n\nSo maybe the fix is: when the arrow function body has a leading comment (or specifically a leading block comment that is on the same line), we should set `body_has_soft_line_break = true`, which means the arrow function doesn\'t have a soft line break after `=>`, and it stays on one line.\n\nBut wait, if I change `body_has_soft_line_break` to true, the formatting uses `[space(), format_body]` without any group. That means the arrow will always be on one line after `=>`, and if it doesn\'t fit, it\'s the parent\'s responsibility to break (e.g., the call arguments).\n\nThat sounds right! Let me verify by looking at what happens when `body_has_soft_line_break` is true: the arrow function is always `=> body` on one line, and the grouping/parent context decides whether to break before the arrow function or not.\n\nBut for objects/arrays, when there\'s a leading own-line comment, `body_has_soft_line_break` becomes false, which means it uses `soft_line_indent_or_space`. That\'s because the comment forces a break before the body. But for our case, the comment `/** @type {SomeType} */` is NOT an own-line comment - it\'s on the same line as the body. So it should be treated like the body can stay on one line.\n\nSo maybe the fix should be in the `_` (default) case of `body_has_soft_line_break`: we should also check for leading comments on the body and if there\'s a leading comment that is NOT an own-line comment, we should return `true` (body has soft line break, i.e., the body can stay on the same line).\n\nWait, I\'m confusing myself. Let me re-read the variable name and its usage:\n\n- `body_has_soft_line_break = true` → use `[space(), format_body]` → body stays on same line\n- `body_has_soft_line_break = false` → use `soft_line_indent_or_space` → body can break to next line\n\nSo the name means: "the body itself has natural line breaking behavior" (like arrays, objects, arrow functions naturally break into multiple lines). When true, we just put a space and let the body format itself. When false, we add a soft line break opportunity after `=>`.\n\nNow for a `CallExpression` like `visit(child)`, it doesn\'t have natural line breaking behavior (it stays on one line). Now I need to understand: when the arrow function body is a `CallExpression` with a leading comment, should it be groupable?

The answer from the PR description is: NO. When there's a JSDoc inline type cast comment before the body, the arrow function should not be grouped.

But how do we detect this? The comment `/** @type {SomeType} */` is a leading comment on the body expression. We need to check if the body expression has leading comments that would cause issues when grouping.

Actually, let me think about this differently. Maybe the fix should be in the `body_has_soft_line_break` logic. Currently, when the body is a `CallExpression` (or any expression not explicitly listed), `body_has_soft_line_break` is false, which leads to the `soft_line_indent_or_space` path. But maybe when there's a leading comment on the body expression, we should treat it like objects/arrays and use `body_has_soft_line_break = true` (which uses just `space()` then body, without the soft indent).

Wait, let me re-examine. When `body_has_soft_line_break` is true:
```rust
write!(f, [space(), format_body]);
```
This puts a space then the body, without any group. The body is just formatted inline. This means the arrow function body stays on the same line as `=>` if it fits.

When `body_has_soft_line_break` is false:
```rust
group(&format_args!(
    soft_line_indent_or_space(&format_with(|f| {
        // body content
    })),
    ...
))
```
This creates a group that either puts a space (if it fits) or breaks and indents.

The issue is that when the arrow is grouped as a call argument, the `soft_line_indent_or_space` break causes the arrow to break after `=>`. But if `body_has_soft_line_break` were true, it would just use `space()` and the arrow function would stay on one line, and if it doesn't fit in the call, the call arguments would break instead.

So the fix might be: when the arrow function body expression has a leading comment (specifically a block comment like JSDoc), we should set `body_has_soft_line_break` to true, so the arrow function stays on one line.

But wait, that would change behavior for all arrow functions with leading comments, not just JSDoc casts. Let me check what Prettier does with other types of leading comments.

Actually, let me look more at the `body_has_soft_line_break` logic. For objects, arrays, and arrow functions, it checks `!f.comments().has_leading_own_line_comment(body.span().start)`. If there's a leading own-line comment, `body_has_soft_line_break` becomes false (because of the `!`). 

Wait, let me re-read:

```rust
Expression::ArrowFunctionExpression(_)
| Expression::ArrayExpression(_)
| Expression::ObjectExpression(_) => {
    !f.comments().has_leading_own_line_comment(body.span().start)
}
```

If there's NO leading own-line comment, this returns `true` (body has soft line break). If there IS a leading own-line comment, it returns `false`.

For other expressions (like `CallExpression`):
```rust
_ => {
    is_multiline_template_starting_on_same_line(expression, f.source_text())
        || is_huggable_html_embed(expression, f)
}
```
This returns false unless it's a template or HTML embed.

So the issue is: `CallExpression` with a leading comment returns `body_has_soft_line_break = false`, which uses `soft_line_indent_or_space`. This causes the break after `=>`.

But what should happen? Let me check what Prettier does by looking at the PR description again:

Prettier keeps the arrow function on one line. So for Prettier, the arrow function body with a JSDoc comment should NOT break after `=>`. It should stay on the same line.

So maybe the fix is: when the arrow function body has a leading comment (or specifically a leading block comment that is on the same line), we should set `body_has_soft_line_break = true`, which means the arrow function doesn't have a soft line break after `=>`, and it stays on one line.

But wait, if I change `body_has_soft_line_break` to true, the formatting uses `[space(), format_body]` without any group. That means the arrow will always be on one line after `=>`, and if it doesn't fit, it's the parent's responsibility to break (e.g., the call arguments).

That sounds right! Let me verify by looking at what happens when `body_has_soft_line_break` is true: the arrow function is always `=> body` on one line, and the grouping/parent context decides whether to break before the arrow function or not.

But for objects/arrays, when there's a leading own-line comment, `body_has_soft_line_break` becomes false, which means it uses `soft_line_indent_or_space`. That's because the comment forces a break before the body. But for our case, the comment `/** @type {SomeType} */` is NOT an own-line comment - it's on the same line as the body. So it should be treated like the body can stay on one line.

So maybe the fix should be in the `_` (default) case of `body_has_soft_line_break`: we should also check for leading comments on the body and if there's a leading comment that is NOT an own-line comment, we should return `true` (body has soft line break, i.e., the body can stay on the same line).

Wait, I'm confusing myself. Let me re-read the variable name and its usage:

- `body_has_soft_line_break = true` → use `[space(), format_body]` → body stays on same line
- `body_has_soft_line_break = false` → use `soft_line_indent_or_space` → body can break to next line

So the name means: "the body itself has natural line breaking behavior" (like arrays, objects, arrow functions naturally break into multiple lines). When true, we just put a space and let the body format itself. When false, we add a soft line break opportunity after `=>`.

Now for a `CallExpression` like `visit(child)`, it doesn't have natural line breaking behavior (it stays on one line). So `body_has_soft_line_break` is false, and we use `soft_line_indent_or_space`. This is correct for normal cases.

   881	    ///
   882	    /// For getting a mutable reference of the expression inside, use [`AssignmentTarget::get_expression_mut`].
   883	    ///
   884	    /// ## Example
   885	    ///
   886	    /// - returns `a` when called on `a!` in `a! = b`
   887	    /// - returns `None` when called on `a` in `a = b` because there is no inner expression to get
   888	    pub fn get_expression(&self) -> Option<&Expression<'a>> {
   889	        self.as_simple_assignment_target().and_then(SimpleAssignmentTarget::get_expression)
   890	    }

   893	    ///
   894	    /// For getting an immutable reference of the expression inside, use [`AssignmentTarget::get_expression`].
   895	    ///
   896	    /// ## Example
   897	    ///

   899	    /// - returns `None` when called on `a` in `a = b` because there is no inner expression to get
   900	    pub fn get_expression_mut(&mut self) -> Option<&mut Expression<'a>> {

   905	impl<'a> SimpleAssignmentTarget<'a> {
   906	    /// Returns the identifier name of this assignment target if the target is an identifier or
   907	    /// a member expression, or `None` otherwise.
   908	    ///
   909	    /// ## Example
   910	    ///
   911	    /// - returns identifier `a` when called on the left-hand side of `a = b`
   912	    /// - returns identifier `b` when called on the left-hand side of `a.b = b`
   913	    /// - returns `None` when called on the left-hand side of `a[b] = b` because it is not an identifier
   914	    pub fn get_identifier_name(&self) -> Option<&'a str> {
   915	        match self {
   916	            Self::AssignmentTargetIdentifier(ident) => Some(ident.name.as_str()),
   917	            match_member_expression!(Self) => self.to_member_expression().static_property_name(),
   918	            _ => None,
   919	        }
   920	    }
   921	
   922	    /// Returns the expression inside of this assignment target, if applicable, and returns a reference to it.
   923	    ///
   924	    /// ## Example
   925	    ///
   926	    /// - returns `a` when called on `a!` in `a! = b`
   927	    /// - returns `None` when called on `a` in `a = b` because there is no inner expression to get
   928	    pub fn get_expression(&self) -> Option<&Expression<'a>> {
   929	        match self {
   930	            Self::TSAsExpression(expr) => Some(&expr.expression),
   931	            Self::TSSatisfiesExpression(expr) => Some(&expr.expression),
   932	            Self::TSNonNullExpression(expr) => Some(&expr.expression),
   933	            Self::TSTypeAssertion(expr) => Some(&expr.expression),
   934	            _ => None,
   935	        }
   936	    }
   937	
   938	    /// Returns the expression inside of this assignment target, if applicable, and returns a mutable reference to it.
   939	    ///
   940	    /// For getting an immutable reference of the expression inside, use [`SimpleAssignmentTarget::get_expression`].
   941	    ///
   942	    /// ## Example
   943	    ///
   944	    /// - returns `a` when called on `a!` in `a! = b`
   945	    /// - returns `None` when called on `a` in `a = b` because there is no inner expression to get
   946	    pub fn get_expression_mut(&mut self) -> Option<&mut Expression<'a>> {
   947	        match self {
   948	            Self::TSAsExpression(expr) => Some(&mut expr.expression),
   949	            Self::TSSatisfiesExpression(expr) => Some(&mut expr.expression),
   950	            Self::TSNonNullExpression(expr) => Some(&mut expr.expression),
   951	            Self::TSTypeAssertion(expr) => Some(&mut expr.expression),
   952	            _ => None,
   953	        }
   954	    }
   955	}

  1594

  1596	    pub fn get_expression_mut(&mut self) -> Option<&mut Expression<'a>> {
  1597	        if self.expression
  1598	            && let Statement::ExpressionStatement(expr_stmt) = &mut self.body.statements[0]
  1599	        {
  1600	            return Some(&mut expr_stmt.expression);
  1601	        }
  1602	        None
  1603	    }
  1604	
  1605	    /// Returns `true` if this arrow function's body has a `"use strict"` directive.
  1606	    pub fn has_use_strict_directive(&self) -> bool {
  1607	        self.body.has_use_strict_directive()
  1608	    }
  1609	}

2026-05-31 11:47:39,443 - INFO - swea-agent-oxc-project__oxc-20857 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now let me understand the issue better. Let me look at the `body_has_soft_line_break` check at line 169-181 again. The key issue is in the `_` (default) case:

```rust
_ => {
    is_multiline_template_starting_on_same_line(expression, f.source_text())
        || is_huggable_html_embed(expression, f)
}
```

This returns `false` for most expressions, including `CallExpression`. So `body_has_soft_line_break` is `false`, and we use `soft_line_indent_or_space`.

But the real issue is: the arrow function is grouped as a call argument (because `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` returns true), and when grouped, it tries to fit on one line. If it doesn't fit, the `soft_line_indent_or_space` breaks after `=>`.

The Prettier behavior suggests that when the arrow body has a JSDoc inline type cast comment, the arrow should NOT be grouped, so the call arguments are expanded instead.

Let me check if there's a simpler approach: modify `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` to check if the body expression has leading comments that are multi-line block comments (like JSDoc inline type casts).

Actually, looking at the Prettier source (from memory), the check is about whether the arrow function body is "simple" enough. An inline JSDoc comment makes it not simple.

Let me look at the `can_group_expression_argument` function again and see if we can add a check for leading comments on the arrow function body.

In `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument`, after determining that the body expression is a `CallExpression` (which is groupable), we should also check if the body has leading comments that would make it not groupable.

Let me check how to access the body's first statement's leading comments:

The body is `&arrow_function.body` which is a `FunctionBody`. The first statement is an `ExpressionStatement`. We need to check if there are leading comments on this expression statement (or on its expression).

Actually, the comment `/** @type {SomeType} */` is attached to the expression `visit(child)` (the CallExpression). In the AST, after removing `ParenthesizedExpression`, the comment becomes a leading comment of the `CallExpression`.

So in `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument`, we could check if the body expression has leading comments. But we have access to `f: &Formatter` and we can check `f.comments().has_comment_before(body_expression_span)`.

But wait, `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` takes `arrow_function: &ArrowFunctionExpression` (not `AstNode`). We don't have the span of the body expression directly? Actually we do: `arrow_function.body.span` gives the body span, and the first statement's expression span can be accessed.

Let me look at how to get the span of the body expression:

For an arrow function with `expression: true`, the body is a `FunctionBody` with one `ExpressionStatement`. The expression of that statement is what we want to check for leading comments.

Let me now write a fix. I'll modify `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` to check if the body expression has leading comments (specifically block comments that are on the same line, like JSDoc inline type casts).

But first, let me check if there's a more general principle: maybe we should not group ANY arrow function whose body expression has leading comments? Let me check what Prettier does:

Looking at the Prettier source (https://github.com/prettier/prettier/blob/main/src/language-js/print/call-arguments.js), there's a function `canGroupArrowFunctionExpressionArgument` that checks:

```js
function canGroupArrowFunctionExpressionArgument(arrowFunction, isArrowRecursion) {
  // ...
  if (body.type === "CallExpression" || body.type === "ConditionalExpression") {
    return !isArrowRecursion;
  }
  // ...
}
```

And then there's also `shouldGroupLast` which checks:

```js
function shouldGroupLast(args) {
  // ...
  if (last.type === "ArrowFunctionExpression") {
    if (last.body.type === "CallExpression" || ...) {
      return true;
    }
  }
  // ...
}
```

But Prettier also has a check for comments. Let me look at the Prettier issue that might be related. 

Actually, I think the fix might be simpler. Let me check if the issue is that the arrow function is grouped, but then when formatting the grouped arrow function, the leading comment on the body causes a break after `=>`. Maybe the fix should be in how the arrow body is formatted when it's a grouped call argument.

Let me check the `format` method again, specifically lines 183-216:

```rust
if body_has_soft_line_break {
    write!(f, [space(), format_body]);
} else {
    // ...
    write!(
        f,
        group(&format_args!(
            soft_line_indent_or_space(&format_with(|f| {
                // ...
                write!(f, format_body);
                // ...
            })),
            // ...
        ))
    );
}
```

When `body_has_soft_line_break` is false (which is the case for `CallExpression`), we wrap the body in `soft_line_indent_or_space`. This means: if it fits on the current line, just add a space; if not, break and indent.

The problem is that when the arrow is a grouped call argument, the formatter tries to make it fit on one line. But the body with the JSDoc comment is too long. The `soft_line_indent_or_space` then breaks after `=>`.

What if we change the logic so that when the body expression has a leading comment (specifically a same-line block comment), we treat it like `body_has_soft_line_break = true`? That way, the arrow function doesn't have a break opportunity after `=>`, and if it doesn't fit in the call, the call arguments expand instead.

Let me look at this approach:

In the `body_has_soft_line_break` computation, for the default case (`_`), we could add a check for leading comments:

```rust
_ => {
    is_multiline_template_starting_on_same_line(expression, f.source_text())
        || is_huggable_html_embed(expression, f)
        || has_leading_inline_comment(expression, body, f)
}
```

Where `has_leading_inline_comment` checks if the body expression has leading comments that are block comments on the same line.

But this might be too broad. Let me look at what exactly Prettier does.

Let me search for any existing handling of this in the codebase:

    10	/// Checks if a node is a type cast node and returns the comments to be printed.
    11	///
    12	/// This function detects if a node is part of a TypeScript type cast pattern
    13	/// by checking for JSDoc type cast comments and proper parenthesis structure.
    14	///
    15	/// Returns:
    16	/// - `Some(&[])` if the node is a type cast node but no comments need to be printed
    17	/// - `Some(&[Comment, ...])` if the node is a type cast node with comments to print
    18	/// - `None` if the node is not a type cast node
    19	pub fn is_type_cast_node<'a>(node: &impl GetSpan, f: &Formatter<'_, 'a>) -> Option<&'a [Comment]> {
    20	    let comments = f.context().comments();
    21	    let span = node.span();
    22	    let source = f.source_text();
    23	
    24	    // Check if there's a closing parenthesis after the node (possibly after comments)
    25	    if !source.next_non_whitespace_byte_is(span.end, b')') {
    26	        let comments_after_node = comments.comments_after(span.end);
    27	        let mut start = span.end;
    28	        // Skip comments after the node to find the next non-whitespace byte whether it's a `)`
    29	        for comment in comments_after_node {
    30	            if !source.bytes_range(start, comment.span.start).trim_ascii_start().is_empty() {
    31	                break;
    32	            }
    33	            start = comment.span.end;
    34	        }
    35	        // Still not a `)`, return early because it's not a type cast
    36	        if !source.next_non_whitespace_byte_is(start, b')') {
    37	            return None;
    38	        }
    39	    }
    40	
    41	    // Check for type cast comment in printed or unprinted comments
    42	    if !comments.is_handled_type_cast_comment()
    43	        && let Some(last_printed_comment) = comments.printed_comments().last()
    44	        && last_printed_comment.span.end <= span.start
    45	        && source.next_non_whitespace_byte_is(last_printed_comment.span.end, b'(')
    46	        && f.comments().is_type_cast_comment(last_printed_comment)
    47	    {
    48	        // Get the source text from the end of type cast comment to the node span
    49	        let node_source_text = source.bytes_range(last_printed_comment.span.end, span.end);
    50	
    51	        // `(/** @type {Number} */ (bar).zoo)`
    52	        //                         ^^^^
    53	        // Should wrap for `baz` rather than `baz.zoo`
    54	        if has_closed_parentheses(node_source_text) {
    55	            None
    56	        } else {
    57	            // Type cast node, but comment was already printed
    58	            Some(&[])
    59	        }
    60	    } else if let Some(type_cast_comment_index) = comments.get_type_cast_comment_index(span) {
    61	        let comments = f.context().comments().unprinted_comments();
    62	        let type_cast_comment = &comments[type_cast_comment_index];
    63	
    64	        // Get the source text from the end of type cast comment to the node span
    65	        let node_source_text = source.bytes_range(type_cast_comment.span.end, span.end);
    66	
    67	        // `(/** @type {Number} */ (bar).zoo)`
    68	        //                         ^^^^
    69	        // Should wrap for `baz` rather than `baz.zoo`
    70	        if has_closed_parentheses(node_source_text) {
    71	            None
    72	        } else {
    73	            // Type cast node with comments to print
    74	            Some(&comments[..=type_cast_comment_index])
    75	        }
    76	    } else {
    77	        // No typecast comment
    78	        None
    79	    }
    80	}
    81	
    82	/// Formats a node with TypeScript type cast comments if present.
    83	///
    84	/// This function handles the formatting of JSDoc type cast comments that appear
    85	/// immediately before parenthesized expressions, creating patterns like:
    86	/// `(/** @type {string} */ value)` or `(/** @type {number} */ (expression))`
    87	///
    88	/// The function:
    89	/// 1. Checks if there's a closing parenthesis after the node (indicating a type cast)
    90	/// 2. Looks for associated type cast comments that precede the node
    91	/// 3. Wraps the node in parentheses with proper formatting and indentation
    92	/// 4. Handles both object/array expressions and other expression types differently
    93	///
    94	/// Returns `Ok(true)` if the node was formatted as a type cast, `Ok(false)` otherwise.
    95	/// This allows callers to know whether they need to apply their own formatting.
    96	pub fn format_type_cast_comment_node<'a, T>(
    97	    node: &(impl Format<'a, T> + GetSpan),
    98	    is_object_or_array_expression: bool,
    99	    f: &mut Formatter<'_, 'a>,
   100	) -> bool {
   101	    // Check if this is a type cast node and get the comments to print
   102	    let Some(type_cast_comments) = is_type_cast_node(node, f) else {
   103	        return false;
   104	    };
   105	
   106	    // Print the type cast comments if any
   107	    if !type_cast_comments.is_empty() {
   108	        write!(f, [FormatLeadingComments::Comments(type_cast_comments)]);
   109	    }
   110	
   111	    let span = node.span();
   112	    f.context_mut().comments_mut().mark_as_type_cast_node(node);
   113	
   114	    // https://github.com/prettier/prettier/blob/7584432401a47a26943dd7a9ca9a8e032ead7285/src/language-js/print/estree.js#L117-L120
   115	    if is_object_or_array_expression && !f.comments().has_comment_before(span.start) {
   116	        write!(f, group(&format_args!("(", &format_with(|f| node.fmt(f)), ")")));
   117	    } else {
   118	        write!(f, group(&format_args!("(", soft_block_indent(&format_with(|f| node.fmt(f))), ")")));
   119	    }
   120	
   121	    true
   122	}
   123	
   124	/// Check if the source text has properly closed parentheses starting with '('.
   125	/// Returns true if the text starts with '(' and all parentheses are balanced.
   126	fn has_closed_parentheses(source: &[u8]) -> bool {
   127	    let mut paren_count = 0i32;
   128	    let mut i = 0;
   129	
   130	    while i < source.len() {
   131	        match source[i] {
   132	            b'(' => paren_count += 1,
   133	            b')' => {
   134	                paren_count -= 1;
   135	                if paren_count == 0 {
   136	                    return true;
   137	                }
   138	            }
   139	            b'/' if i + 1 < source.len() => {
   140	                match source[i + 1] {
   141	                    b'/' => {
   142	                        // Skip to end of line comment
   143	                        i += 2;
   144	                        while i < source.len() && source[i] != b'\n' {
   145	                            i += 1;
   146	                        }
   147	                        continue;
   148	                    }
   149	                    b'*' => {
   150	                        // Skip to end of block comment
   151	                        i += 2;
   152	                        while i + 1 < source.len() {
   153	                            if source[i] == b'*' && source[i + 1] == b'/' {
   154	                                i += 2;
   155	                                break;
   156	                            }
   157	                            i += 1;
   158	                        }
   159	                        continue;
   160	                    }
   161	                    _ => {}
   162	                }
   163	            }
   164	            quote @ (b'"' | b'\'' | b'`') => {
   165	                // Skip string literal (double-quoted, single-quoted, or template)
   166	                i += 1;
   167	                while i < source.len() {
   168	                    match source[i] {
   169	                        b if b == quote => break,
   170	                        b'\\' if i + 1 < source.len() => i += 1, // Skip escaped character
   171	                        _ => {}
   172	                    }
   173	                    i += 1;
   174	                }
   175	            }
   176	            _ => {}
   177	        }
   178	        i += 1;
   179	    }
   180	
   181	    // Return true only if parentheses are properly balanced
   182	    paren_count == 0
   183	}
   184

So it stays.\n\nFor the second example:\n```\nconst body = transform_body(state.analysis.instance_body, b.id("$.run"), (node) =>\n  /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node)),\n);\n```\nThe line `transform_body(state.analysis.instance_body, b.id("$.run"), (node) => /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node)))` is longer than 100 chars, so it breaks.\n\nBut Prettier breaks differently:\n```\nconst body = transform_body(\n\tstate.analysis.instance_body,\n\tb.id(\'$.run\'),\n\t(node) => /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node))\n);\n```\n\nSo Prettier expands the call arguments rather than breaking after `=>`.\n\nThe reason is that Prettier\'s `shouldGroupLast` returns `false` for arrow functions whose body has comments. 2026-05-31 11:48:25,051 - INFO - swea-agent-oxc-project__oxc-20857 - 💭 THOUGHT
This is very relevant! The `typecast.rs` file has utilities for handling JSDoc type cast comments. But it seems like this is used for formatting type cast expressions like `(/** @type {string} */ value)`.

Now, the issue is about arrow functions with JSDoc type casts in their body. The arrow function body `/** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))` contains:
1. A JSDoc comment `/** @type {SomeType} */`
2. A parenthesized expression `(visit(child))`

After parsing, the parenthesized expression is removed (since `preserve_parens` is disabled), and the comment becomes a leading comment of `visit(child)`.

So the arrow body is just a `CallExpression` (`visit(child)`) with a leading block comment. 

Now, the question is: why does Oxfmt break after `=>` instead of keeping the arrow on one line?

Let me trace through the formatting:

1. The arrow function `(child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))` is an argument to `items.map()`.
2. `can_group_expression_argument` is called, which calls `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument`.
3. `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` returns `true` because the body is a `CallExpression`.
4. The arrow is formatted as a grouped argument.
5. In `FormatJsArrowFunctionExpression::format`, `body_has_soft_line_break` is `false` (since body is a `CallExpression`).
6. The else branch uses `soft_line_indent_or_space` to format the body.
7. The body content includes the leading comment `/** @type {SomeType} */` followed by `visit(child)`.
8. If the whole thing fits on one line, it stays. But if it doesn't fit (like in the second example with printWidth 100), the `soft_line_indent_or_space` breaks after `=>`.

But wait, for the first example at printWidth 100:
```
const result = items.map((child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child)));
```
This fits on one line (the whole line is within 100 chars). So it stays.

For the second example:
```
const body = transform_body(state.analysis.instance_body, b.id("$.run"), (node) =>
  /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node)),
);
```
The line `transform_body(state.analysis.instance_body, b.id("$.run"), (node) => /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node)))` is longer than 100 chars, so it breaks.

But Prettier breaks differently:
```
const body = transform_body(
	state.analysis.instance_body,
	b.id('$.run'),
	(node) => /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node))
);
```

So Prettier expands the call arguments rather than breaking after `=>`.

The reason is that Prettier's `shouldGroupLast` returns `false` for arrow functions whose body has comments. Or, the grouping logic works differently.

Let me look at the `should_group_last_argument_impl` and `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` more carefully.

I think the fix should be in `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument`: we should check if the body expression has leading comments that are block comments (like JSDoc inline type casts). If it does, the arrow should not be groupable.

But actually, a more general approach: maybe we should check if the body has any leading comments at all? Let me check what Prettier does.

Let me search for Prettier's logic:

Actually, I think I should look at this from a different angle. Let me look at the `body_has_soft_line_break` check. For objects, arrays, and arrow functions, there's a special check:

```rust
Expression::ArrowFunctionExpression(_)
| Expression::ArrayExpression(_)
| Expression::ObjectExpression(_) => {
    !f.comments().has_leading_own_line_comment(body.span().start)
}
```

If there's NO leading own-line comment, `body_has_soft_line_break` is `true`. If there IS a leading own-line comment, it's `false`.

For other expressions (like `CallExpression`), there's no such check. So the comment on the body doesn't affect `body_has_soft_line_break`.

Maybe the fix should be: for the default case (`_`), also check if there's a leading comment (not necessarily own-line) on the body expression. If there is, set `body_has_soft_line_break = true` so the arrow function stays on one line.

But wait, that would change behavior for ALL leading comments, not just JSDoc inline type casts. Let me check if that's desirable.

Let me look at the Prettier behavior for other leading comments on arrow body:

For example:
```js
const x = items.map((child) =>
  // some comment
  visit(child)
);
```

In this case, the comment is a line comment (own-line), which forces a break after `=>`. That's correct.

But for:
```js
const x = items.map((child) => /* some comment */ visit(child));
```

Here the comment is a block comment on the same line. What does Prettier do? According to the PR, the JSDoc inline type cast is a special case where Prettier keeps the arrow on one line. But I'm not sure about general block comments.

Let me check the Prettier source code. Actually, I think the key difference is that the JSDoc type cast is a `/** @type {X} */` comment which is recognized as a type cast by Prettier. Prettier has special handling for these.

Looking at the `typecast.rs` file, there's already logic for detecting type cast comments. The function `is_type_cast_node` checks if a node is part of a type cast pattern. Maybe we can use this in `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` to determine if the arrow body has a type cast comment.

But actually, I think the issue might be simpler. Let me look at the `body_has_soft_line_break` logic again:

For objects/arrays/arrow functions, when there's NO leading own-line comment, `body_has_soft_line_break` is `true`, meaning the body stays on the same line as `=>`. When there IS a leading own-line comment, it's `false`, meaning the body breaks after `=>`.

For other expressions (like `CallExpression`), the logic doesn't consider leading comments at all. But maybe it should? If the body expression has a leading comment that is NOT an own-line comment (i.e., it's on the same line), then `body_has_soft_line_break` should be `true` (stay on same line). If it has an own-line comment, it should be `false` (break after `=>`).

Wait, that's exactly the same logic as for objects/arrays/arrow functions. Let me check:

For objects/arrays/arrow functions:
- No leading own-line comment → `true` (body has soft line break = stays on same line)
- Has leading own-line comment → `false` (break after `=>`)

For other expressions (like `CallExpression`):
- Currently always `false` (unless template/html)

But maybe it should be:
- No leading comment → `false` (use soft_line_indent_or_space)
- Has leading comment that is NOT own-line → `true` (stay on same line, like objects/arrays without comments)
- Has leading own-line comment → `false` (break after `=>`)

Wait, that doesn't make sense either. Let me think about this differently.

The `body_has_soft_line_break` variable determines whether to use `[space(), format_body]` (true) or `soft_line_indent_or_space` (false).

When `body_has_soft_line_break` is true:
- The body is placed directly after `=> ` with no break opportunity.
- The body's own formatting determines if it breaks.
- For objects/arrays/arrow functions, the body naturally breaks into multiple lines.

When `body_has_soft_line_break` is false:
- A soft line break is added after `=>`.
- If the content fits, it stays on one line; if not, it breaks after `=>`.

Now for a `CallExpression` body like `visit(child)`, it doesn't naturally break. So `body_has_soft_line_break` is false, and we rely on `soft_line_indent_or_space` to break if needed.

But when there's a leading comment like `/** @type {SomeType} */ `, the body content becomes longer. The `soft_line_indent_or_space` may cause a break after `=>` if the whole thing doesn't fit.

The Prettier behavior suggests that when there's a JSDoc inline type cast, the arrow should stay on one line (like objects/arrays without leading comments). So maybe we should treat `CallExpression` with a leading JSDoc type cast comment like an object/array without leading comment → `body_has_soft_line_break = true`.

But how do we detect a JSDoc type cast comment? We can check if there's a leading comment that is a block comment containing `@type`.

Actually, let me look at the comment handling more broadly. The `is_type_cast_comment` function exists:

   413	    pub fn is_type_cast_comment(&self, comment: &Comment) -> bool {
   414	        const TYPE_PATTERN: &[u8] = b"@type";
   415	        const SATISFIES_PATTERN: &[u8] = b"@satisfies";
   416	
   417	        /// Checks if a pattern matches at the given position.
   418	        fn matches_pattern_at(bytes: &[u8], pos: usize, pattern: &[u8]) -> bool {
   419	            bytes[pos..].starts_with(pattern)
   420	                && bytes
   421	                    .get(pos + pattern.len())
   422	                    .is_some_and(|&byte| byte.is_ascii_whitespace() || byte == b'{')
   423	        }
   424	
   425	        if !matches!(comment.content, CommentContent::Jsdoc) {
   426	            return false;
   427	        }
   428	
   429	        let bytes = self.source_text.text_for(&comment.span).as_bytes();
   430	        for (i, &byte) in bytes.iter().enumerate() {
   431	            if byte == b'@'
   432	                && (matches_pattern_at(bytes, i, TYPE_PATTERN)
   433	                    || matches_pattern_at(bytes, i, SATISFIES_PATTERN))
   434	            {
   435	                return true;
   436	            }
   437	        }
   438	        false
   439	    }
   440	
   441	    /// Finds the index of a type cast comment before the given span.
   442	    ///
   443	    /// Searches for a JSDoc comment containing @type or @satisfies that is followed
   444	    /// by an opening parenthesis, which indicates a type cast pattern.
   445	    pub fn get_type_cast_comment_index(&self, span: Span) -> Option<usize> {
   446	        self.unprinted_comments().iter().take_while(|c| c.span.end <= span.start).position(
   447	            |comment| {
   448	                self.source_text.next_non_whitespace_byte_is(comment.span.end, b'(')
   449	                    && self.is_type_cast_comment(comment)
   450	            },
   451	        )
   452	    }
   453	
   454	    /// Marks the given span as a type cast node.
   455	    pub fn mark_as_type_cast_node(&mut self, node: &impl GetSpan) {
   456	        self.type_cast_node_span = node.span();
   457	        self.last_handled_type_cast_comment = self.printed_count;
   458	    }
   459	
   460	    /// Checks if the most recently printed type cast comment has been handled.
   461	    pub fn is_handled_type_cast_comment(&self) -> bool {
   462	        self.printed_count == self.last_handled_type_cast_comment
   463	    }

2026-05-31 11:48:49,861 - INFO - swea-agent-oxc-project__oxc-20857 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now I have a good understanding. The `is_type_cast_comment` function recognizes JSDoc comments that contain `@type` or `@satisfies`. This is used to detect type cast patterns like `/** @type {string} */ (value)`.

Now, back to the arrow function issue. The problem is in `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument`. When the arrow function body has a type cast comment (JSDoc inline type cast), the arrow function should NOT be grouped as a call argument. 

Let me check if there's a way to detect if the body expression has a leading type cast comment. We can check `f.comments().has_comment_before(body_expression_span)` and then check if any of those comments are type cast comments.

But actually, looking at this more carefully, I think the fix should be in `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` or in the `body_has_soft_line_break` logic.

Let me look at the Prettier source more carefully. In Prettier's `canGroupArrowFunctionExpressionArgument`:

```js
function canGroupArrowFunctionExpressionArgument(arrowFunction, isArrowRecursion) {
  const body = arrowFunction.body;
  // ...
  if (
    body.type === "ObjectExpression" ||
    body.type === "ArrayExpression" ||
    body.type === "JSXElement" ||
    body.type === "JSXFragment"
  ) {
    return true;
  }
  if (body.type === "ArrowFunctionExpression") {
    return canGroupArrowFunctionExpressionArgument(body, true);
  }
  if (body.type === "ChainExpression") {
    return body.expression.type === "CallExpression" && !isArrowRecursion;
  }
  if (
    body.type === "CallExpression" ||
    body.type === "ConditionalExpression"
  ) {
    return !isArrowRecursion;
  }
  return false;
}
```

This is exactly what Oxfmt implements. But there's also `shouldGroupLast` in Prettier which has additional checks:

```js
function shouldGroupLast(args) {
  const lastArg = args[args.length - 1];
  // ...
  if (lastArg.type === "ArrowFunctionExpression") {
    return canGroupArrowFunctionExpressionArgument(lastArg, false);
  }
  // ...
}
```

And there's also `shouldGroupFirst` which is similar.

So Prettier would ALSO group this arrow function. But Prettier's formatting of the grouped arrow function might be different.

Let me look at how Prettier formats the arrow function when it's the last argument. In Prettier, when the last argument is grouped, it uses a different layout that tries harder to keep it on one line. Specifically, Prettier uses `shouldGroupLast` which triggers `group` with `shouldBreak: false` initially, and if it doesn't fit, the whole call expands.

The key difference might be in how the arrow function body is formatted when it's a grouped argument. In Oxfmt, when an arrow function is a grouped argument, it still uses `soft_line_indent_or_space` for the body. In Prettier, the logic might be different.

Let me look at how Oxfmt formats grouped arrow function arguments. In `write_grouped_arguments` (line 637-750), when an arrow function is grouped:

1. First, all arguments are pre-formatted to check if they break.
2. If non-grouped arguments break, all arguments are expanded.
3. Otherwise, the grouped argument (arrow function) is formatted with `FormatJsArrowFunctionExpression` with `cache_mode: Cache`.

The arrow function formatting still uses the same `format` method, which still uses `soft_line_indent_or_space` for the body.

But wait, there's also the `call_argument_layout` option that's passed to `FormatJsArrowFunctionExpressionOptions`. In `write_grouped_arguments`, the options are:

```rust
FormatJsArrowFunctionExpressionOptions {
    cache_mode: FunctionCacheMode::Cache,
    ..FormatJsArrowFunctionExpressionOptions::default()
},
```

The `call_argument_layout` is not set (default is `None`). But in the `format` method, there's logic that checks `call_argument_layout`:

```rust
let is_last_call_arg = matches!(
    self.options.call_argument_layout,
    Some(GroupedCallArgumentLayout::GroupedLastArgument)
);
```

This is used to determine if a trailing comma and soft line break should be added after the arrow function. But it's not set here because the arrow function is being formatted as part of `write_grouped_arguments`, not from the call arguments directly.

Actually, the issue might be different. Let me trace through the code more carefully.

When we have `transform_body(state.analysis.instance_body, b.id('$.run'), (node) => /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node)))`:

1. The call arguments are `[state.analysis.instance_body, b.id('$.run'), (node) => /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node))]`
2. `arguments_grouped_layout` is called. Since there are 3 arguments (not 2), it goes to the else branch:
   ```rust
   should_group_last_argument(args, f)
       .then_some(GroupedCallArgumentLayout::GroupedLastArgument)
   ```
3. `should_group_last_argument` checks if the last argument can be grouped. It calls `can_group_expression_argument` on the arrow function, which calls `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument`, which returns `true` (body is a `CallExpression`).
4. `should_group_last_argument_impl` also checks various conditions and returns `true`.
5. So the arrow function IS grouped as the last argument.
6. `write_grouped_arguments` is called with `GroupedCallArgumentLayout::GroupedLastArgument`.
7. In `write_grouped_arguments`, all arguments are pre-formatted. The arrow function is formatted with `FormatJsArrowFunctionExpression`.
8. After pre-formatting, if non-grouped arguments don't break, it proceeds to format the grouped version.

The issue is that the arrow function, when formatted, breaks after `=>` because the body with the JSDoc comment is too long. This causes `grouped_breaks` to be true.

2026-05-31 11:49:06,989 - DEBUG - swea-lm-oxc-project__oxc-20857 - Response: ModelResponse(id='722cd0eb340244d0aff89d28cf423bc8', created=1780228132, model='DeepSeek-V4-Pro', object='chat.completion', system_fingerprint=None, choices=[Choices(finish_reason='tool_calls', index=0, message=Message(content="This is complex. Let me step back and think about the simplest fix.\n\nThe PR description says:\n> When an arrow function body starts with a JSDoc inline type cast (`/** @type {X} */`), Prettier breaks after the opening parenthesis and keeps the entire arrow expression on one line, while Oxfmt breaks after `=>` and puts the JSDoc type cast + body on the next line.\n\nSo the fix should make Oxfmt behave like Prettier: keep the arrow function on one line, and if it doesn't fit, break the call arguments (not the arrow function).\n\nThe most direct fix would be to NOT group arrow functions whose body has a leading JSDoc type cast comment.
