2026-05-31 11:49:06,996 - INFO - swea-agent-oxc-project__oxc-20857 - 💭 THOUGHT
This is complex. Let me step back and think about the simplest fix.

The PR description says:
> When an arrow function body starts with a JSDoc inline type cast (`/** @type {X} */`), Prettier breaks after the opening parenthesis and keeps the entire arrow expression on one line, while Oxfmt breaks after `=>` and puts the JSDoc type cast + body on the next line.

So the fix should make Oxfmt behave like Prettier: keep the arrow function on one line, and if it doesn't fit, break the call arguments (not the arrow function).

The most direct fix would be to NOT group arrow functions whose body has a leading JSDoc type cast comment. If the arrow function is not grouped, the call arguments will be formatted with the default `soft_block_indent` layout, which will break after the opening paren if needed.

Let me modify `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` to check for leading type cast comments on the body expression.

First, let me figure out how to access the body expression's span to check for leading comments. In `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument`, we have `arrow_function: &ArrowFunctionExpression`. The body is `&arrow_function.body` (a `FunctionBody`). For an expression arrow, the first statement is an `ExpressionStatement` whose expression is what we want.

But wait, `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` already calls `arrow_function.get_expression()` which returns `Some(&Expression)` for expression arrows. The expression is the body expression (e.g., `visit(child)`). But we need its span to check for leading comments.

However, we don't have the `AstNode` wrapper here, so we can't easily use `f.comments().has_comment_before()`. But we can use the raw span from `expr.span()`.

Let me check if `Expression` has a `span()` method

2026-05-31 11:49:48,691 - INFO - swea-agent-oxc-project__oxc-20857 - 💭 THOUGHT
Good, `Expression` implements `GetSpan`. So we can get the span of the body expression.

Now let me modify `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` to check for leading type cast comments on the body expression.

But actually, I realize I need to think about this differently. The issue might not be about `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` at all. The arrow function IS groupable - it should be grouped. The issue is that when it's grouped and the body has a JSDoc comment, the arrow function breaks after `=>` instead of staying on one line.

Let me look at this from the `body_has_soft_line_break` perspective again. Maybe the fix is simpler: when the arrow function body has a leading comment that is a block comment on the same line (like JSDoc inline type cast), we should treat `body_has_soft_line_break` as `true`, which means the arrow function stays on one line.

Let me check: if `body_has_soft_line_break` is `true`, the formatting is:
```rust
write!(f, [space(), format_body]);
```

This just puts a space then the body, without any break opportunity. If the arrow function is a grouped argument, the call arguments group would then be responsible for breaking if the whole thing doesn't fit. And since the arrow function doesn't have a break opportunity, the only way to break is to expand the call arguments, which is exactly what Prettier does.

So the fix should be in the `body_has_soft_line_break` logic. Let me modify the default case to also check for leading comments on the body expression.

Actually, let me look at the condition more carefully:

```rust
let body_has_soft_line_break =
    arrow_expression.is_none_or(|expression| match expression {
        Expression::ArrowFunctionExpression(_)
        | Expression::ArrayExpression(_)
        | Expression::ObjectExpression(_) => {
            !f.comments().has_leading_own_line_comment(body.span().start)
        }
        Expression::JSXElement(_) | Expression::JSXFragment(_) => true,
        _ => {
            is_multiline_template_starting_on_same_line(expression, f.source_text())
                || is_huggable_html_embed(expression, f)
        }
    });
```

For `Expression::ArrowFunctionExpression`, `Expression::ArrayExpression`, `Expression::ObjectExpression`, the check is:
- If there's NO leading own-line comment → `true` (body stays on same line)
- If there IS a leading own-line comment → `false` (break after `=>`)

For other expressions, there's no check for leading comments. But maybe we should add a similar check: if there's a leading comment that is NOT an own-line comment (i.e., it's on the same line), then `body_has_soft_line_break` should be `true` (stay on same line). If there's a leading own-line comment, it should be `false` (break after `=>`).

Wait, but for most expressions without any leading comments, `body_has_soft_line_break` is `false`, which is correct (they use `soft_line_indent_or_space`). If we add a check that makes it `true` when there's a same-line leading comment, that would change behavior for all expressions with same-line leading comments.

Let me check what the expected behavior is for a simple case like:
```js
const x = items.map((child) => /* comment */ visit(child));
```

What does Prettier do with this? I'm not sure. But the PR is specifically about JSDoc inline type casts.

Maybe the fix should be more targeted: check if the leading comment is a JSDoc type cast comment (contains `@type` or `@satisfies`). If so, treat the body like an object/array without leading comment (i.e., `body_has_soft_line_break = true`).

But actually, I wonder if there's an even simpler fix. Let me look at the `format` method of `FormatJsArrowFunctionExpression` again, specifically lines 167-216.

The `body_has_soft_line_break` check could be extended for the default case to also check if there's a leading block comment that is on the same line. If there is, the body should stay on the same line as `=>` (like objects/arrays).

Let me look at how `has_leading_own_line_comment` works:

From `comments.rs`:
```rust
pub fn has_leading_own_line_comment(&self, start: u32) -> bool {
    self.comments_before_iter(start).any(|comment| comment.followed_by_newline())
}
```

This checks if any comment before the given position is followed by a newline (i.e., is on its own line).

For our case, the comment `/** @type {SomeType} */` is NOT followed by a newline (it's on the same line as the expression). So `has_leading_own_line_comment` would return `false`.

If we apply the same logic as for objects/arrays:
```rust
!f.comments().has_leading_own_line_comment(body.span().start)
```
This would return `true` (no own-line comment → body has soft line break → stay on same line).

But we also need to check if there's a leading comment at all. If there's no leading comment, the current behavior (using `soft_line_indent_or_space`) is correct.

So maybe the fix for the `_` case should be:
```rust
_ => {
    if f.comments().has_comment_before(body.span().start) {
        !f.comments().has_leading_own_line_comment(body.span().start)
    } else {
        is_multiline_template_starting_on_same_line(expression, f.source_text())
            || is_huggable_html_embed(expression, f)
    }
}
```

Wait, but this would make ANY expression with a same-line leading comment have `body_has_soft_line_break = true`. That might not be correct for all cases.

Let me check what Prettier does with a simple block comment before an expression in an arrow function body:

```js
const x = items.map((child) => /* comment */ visit(child));
```

I think Prettier would format this as:
```js
const x = items.map((child) => /* comment */ visit(child));
```
(on one line if it fits)

And if it doesn't fit:
```js
const x = items.map(
  (child) => /* comment */ visit(child)
);
```

So Prettier would break the call arguments, not the arrow function. That's the same as for JSDoc type casts.

So maybe my proposed fix is correct: when the body expression has a leading comment that is NOT an own-line comment, treat it like objects/arrays without leading comments (i.e., `body_has_soft_line_break = true`).

But wait, there's a subtlety. For objects/arrays, the reason `body_has_soft_line_break` is `true` when there's no leading own-line comment is that objects/arrays have their own natural line breaking strategy (they become blocks). For other expressions like `CallExpression`, there's no natural line breaking. But if we set `body_has_soft_line_break = true`, the body is just placed after `=> ` without any break opportunity. If the body doesn't fit on the line, the parent (call arguments) must break.

This is actually the desired behavior for the JSDoc type cast case! The arrow function should stay on one line, and if it doesn't fit in the call, the call arguments should break.

But what about a case like:
```js
const x = (child) => /* comment */ visit(child);
```
(not as a call argument)

If `body_has_soft_line_break` is `true`, it would format as:
```js
const x = (child) => /* comment */ visit(child);
```
(no break opportunity after `=>`)

And if it doesn't fit within the print width, it would... actually, the whole assignment would need to break. Let me check what happens:

The assignment formatting in `assignment_like.rs` handles this. It would likely break after `=`:
```js
const x =
  (child) => /* comment */ visit(child);
```

That seems fine.

But what if the expression is very long? Without `soft_line_indent_or_space`, there's no way to break after `=>`. The only break opportunity is before the arrow function (from the assignment or call). That might lead to very long lines if the parent doesn't have a break opportunity either.

However, looking at the PR description, this is exactly what Prettier does: it keeps the arrow function on one line and breaks the parent construct instead.

Let me implement the fix and test it.

Actually, let me first check if there's a simpler approach: maybe we should just not group arrow functions that have leading comments on their body expression. Let me check what happens if we don't group:

If the arrow function is NOT grouped, the call arguments are formatted with the default layout (soft_block_indent). The arguments can break between them. The arrow function itself would still be formatted with `FormatJsArrowFunctionExpression`, which would use `soft_line_indent_or_space` for the body. But now the arrow function is not hugged to the call; it can break after `=>` if needed.

Hmm, but that might still break after `=>` instead of after the opening paren. Let me check by temporarily modifying the code to not group arrow functions with leading comments.

Actually, let me first write a simple test to understand the current behavior better and then test potential fixes.

Let me look more at the `body_has_soft_line_break` and understand what happens when I change it.

Let me create a patch to test:

First, let me look at the exact lines I need to modify:

```rust
let body_has_soft_line_break =
    arrow_expression.is_none_or(|expression| match expression {
        Expression::ArrowFunctionExpression(_)
        | Expression::ArrayExpression(_)
        | Expression::ObjectExpression(_) => {
            !f.comments().has_leading_own_line_comment(body.span().start)
        }
        Expression::JSXElement(_) | Expression::JSXFragment(_) => true,
        _ => {
            is_multiline_template_starting_on_same_line(expression, f.source_text())
                || is_huggable_html_embed(expression, f)
        }
    });
```

I need to add a check for leading comments in the `_` case. Let me modify the `_` case to:

```rust
_ => {
    // When the body expression has a leading comment that is NOT on its own line,
    // treat it like objects/arrays: keep the body on the same line as `=>`.
    // This matches Prettier's behavior for JSDoc inline type casts like
    // `(child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))`.
    if f.comments().has_comment_before(body.span().start) {
        !f.comments().has_leading_own_line_comment(body.span().start)
    } else {
        is_multiline_template_starting_on_same_line(expression, f.source_text())
            || is_huggable_html_embed(expression, f)
    }
}
```

Wait, but `body.span().start` is the start of the function body, not the start of the expression. The comment is attached to the expression inside the body, not to the body itself. Let me check what span the comment is attached to.

In the original source:
```
(child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))
```

After parsing (with `preserve_parens` disabled), the `ParenthesizedExpression` is removed. The comment `/** @type {SomeType} */` was originally before the `ParenthesizedExpression`. After removal, the comment becomes a leading comment of the inner expression `visit(child)` (the `CallExpression`).

So the comment is before `visit(child)`, which is the body expression. The body (`FunctionBody`) span starts at `{` or at the first statement. For an expression arrow, the body span starts at the expression.

So `body.span().start` should be the start of the expression `visit(child)`, and the comment `/** @type {SomeType} */` should be before that. So `f.comments().has_comment_before(body.span().start)` should return `true`.

Let me verify this by looking at the arrow function expression formatting. In line 174:
```rust
!f.comments().has_leading_own_line_comment(body.span().start)
```

This already uses `body.span().start` to check for leading comments on the body. So I can use the same approach.

The grouped formatting tries to fit everything on one line. The arrow function with `body_has_soft_line_break = true` now has no break opportunity after `=>`. So the only way to break is to expand the call arguments. But wait, it's still breaking after `=>`. Why?

```

```

Let me trace: the arrow function is `(node) => /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node))`. The source has `/** @type {Node} */` followed by `(context.visit(node))`. After parsing, `ParenthesizedExpression` is removed, and the comment becomes a leading comment of `context.visit(node)` (the `CallExpression`).

But `context.visit(node)` is a `CallExpression` with a leading comment. The `CallExpression` span starts at `context`. The comment is before that. So `f.comments().has_comment_before(body.span().start)` should check if there are comments before the body start.

But wait, the comment `/** @type {Node} */` is before `context.visit(node)`, but it might not be attached to the body. It might be attached to the `CallExpression` directly.

Let me check: in the parser, when `preserve_parens` is disabled, the `ParenthesizedExpression` is removed. The comments that were attached to the `ParenthesizedExpression` are transferred to the inner expression. So the comment `/** @type {Node} */` should be a leading comment of the `CallExpression` `context.visit(node)`.

Now, `body.span().start` is the start of the `FunctionBody`. For an expression arrow, the function body contains a single `ExpressionStatement` with the expression. The body span should start at the same position as the expression statement, which is the same as the expression (since there's no leading whitespace or semicolon).

So `body.span().start` should equal the span start of `context.visit(node)`. And `f.comments().has_comment_before(body.span().start)` should return `true` because there's a comment before `context.visit(node)`.

But wait, maybe the comment is not before `body.span().start`? Maybe the comment is attached to the expression inside the body, but its position is after `body.span().start`?

Let me check the actual spans. The arrow function body starts at `{` (for block body) or at the expression (for expression body). The comment is before the expression but inside the body. So the comment's span should be after `body.span().start`? No, the body span starts at the first statement, which is the expression. The comment is before the expression, so it should be before `body.span().start`.

Ah, I think I know. For printWidth 100, the call arguments might be grouped differently. The grouped formatting might try to fit everything on one line, and if the arrow function doesn't have a break opportunity, the group expands. But wait, the output shows it broke after `=>`, which means the break opportunity is still there.

Let me check if there's another path that formats the arrow function body differently when it's a grouped argument. In `write_grouped_arguments`, the arrow function is formatted with `FormatJsArrowFunctionExpression` with `cache_mode: Cache`. But the same `format` method is used, which now has my change. So `body_has_soft_line_break` should be `true`.

Unless... the cache mode causes it to use a cached version from before my change? Let me check.

Actually, `cache_mode: Cache` means the body should be cached. But the `body_has_soft_line_break` decision happens before the body formatting. Let me look at the flow:

1. `FormatJsArrowFunctionExpression::format` is called.
2. It computes `body_has_soft_line_break`.
3. It formats the signature.
4. It formats the body.

The `body_has_soft_line_break` is computed in step 2, before any caching. So the cache shouldn't matter.

But wait, there's another issue. The `write_grouped_arguments` function first pre-formats all arguments to check if they break. The pre-formatting also calls `FormatJsArrowFunctionExpression` which computes `body_has_soft_line_break`. If `body_has_soft_line_break` is `true`, the arrow function uses `[space(), format_body]` without a group. The pre-formatted element would not break (since there's no soft line break). Then `grouped_breaks` would be false. Then the grouped layout would try to fit everything on one line.

But the output shows the arrow function broke after `=>`. So something is still adding a break after `=>`.

Let me check if there's another code path that adds a break after `=>`. Let me look at the `format` method more carefully:

```rust
if body_has_soft_line_break {
    write!(f, [space(), format_body]);
} else {
    // ... soft_line_indent_or_space ...
}
```

If `body_has_soft_line_break` is `true`, it uses `[space(), format_body]`. There's no break opportunity. So the arrow function should stay on one line.

But the output for printWidth 100 shows:
```
const body = transform_body(state.analysis.instance_body, b.id("$.run"), (node) =>
  /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node)),
);
```

This stayed on one line. The body expression here is `visit(child)`. The comment `/** @type {SomeType} */` is before it. `body.span().start` points to `visit(child)`. `has_comment_before` should return `true`. And my change makes `body_has_soft_line_break = true`. So the arrow stays on one line. Good.

For the second example:

  /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node)),

   904	struct FormatGroupedFirstArgument<'a, 'b> {
   905	    argument: &'b AstNode<'a, Argument<'a>>,
   906	}
   907	
   908	impl<'a> Format<'a> for FormatGroupedFirstArgument<'a, '_> {
   909	    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut Formatter<'_, 'a>) {
   910	        match self.argument.as_ast_nodes() {
   911	            // Call the arrow function formatting but explicitly passes the call argument layout down
   912	            // so that the arrow function formatting removes any soft line breaks between parameters and the return type.
   913	            AstNodes::ArrowFunctionExpression(arrow) => {
   914	                FormatJsArrowFunctionExpression::new_with_options(
   915	                    arrow,
   916	                    FormatJsArrowFunctionExpressionOptions {
   917	                        cache_mode: FunctionCacheMode::Cache,
   918	                        call_argument_layout: Some(GroupedCallArgumentLayout::GroupedFirstArgument),
   919	                        ..FormatJsArrowFunctionExpressionOptions::default()
   920	                    },
   921	                )
   922	                .fmt(f);
   923	            }
   924	
   925	            // For all other nodes, use the normal formatting (which already has been cached)
   926	            _ => self.argument.fmt(f),
   927	        }
   928	    }
   929	}
   930	
   931	/// Helper for formatting the last grouped argument (see [should_group_last_argument]).
   932	struct FormatGroupedLastArgument<'a, 'b> {
   933	    /// The argument to format
   934	    argument: &'b AstNode<'a, Argument<'a>>,
   935	    /// Is this the only argument in the arguments list
   936	    is_only: bool,
   937	}
   938	
   939	impl<'a> Format<'a> for FormatGroupedLastArgument<'a, '_> {
   940	    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut Formatter<'_, 'a>) {
   941	        // For function and arrow expressions, re-format the node and pass the argument that it is the
   942	        // last grouped argument. This changes the formatting of parameters, type parameters, and return types
   943	        // to remove any soft line breaks.
   944	        match self.argument.as_ast_nodes() {
   945	            AstNodes::Function(function)
   946	                if !self.is_only || function_has_only_simple_parameters(&function.params) =>
   947	            {
   948	                FormatFunction::new_with_options(
   949	                    function,
   950	                    FormatFunctionOptions {
   951	                        cache_mode: FunctionCacheMode::Cache,
   952	                        call_argument_layout: Some(GroupedCallArgumentLayout::GroupedLastArgument),
   953	                    },
   954	                )
   955	                .fmt(f);
   956	            }
   957	            AstNodes::ArrowFunctionExpression(arrow) => {
   958	                FormatJsArrowFunctionExpression::new_with_options(
   959	                    arrow,
   960	                    FormatJsArrowFunctionExpressionOptions {
   961	                        cache_mode: FunctionCacheMode::Cache,
   962	                        call_argument_layout: Some(GroupedCallArgumentLayout::GroupedLastArgument),
   963	                        ..FormatJsArrowFunctionExpressionOptions::default()
   964	                    },
   965	                )
   966	                .fmt(f);
   967	            }
   968	            _ => self.argument.fmt(f),
   969	        }
   970	    }
   971	}
   972	
   973	fn function_has_only_simple_parameters(params: &FormalParameters<'_>) -> bool {
   974	    has_only_simple_parameters(params, false)
   975	}
   976	
   977	/// Tests if this a simple module import like `import("module-name")` or `require("module-name")`.
   978	pub fn is_simple_module_import(
   979	    arguments: &AstNode<'_, ArenaVec<'_, Argument<'_>>>,
   980	    comments: &Comments,
   981	) -> bool {
   982	    if arguments.len() != 1 {
   983	        return false;
   984	    }
   985	
   986	    match arguments.parent() {
   987	        AstNodes::ImportExpression(_) => {}
   988	        AstNodes::CallExpression(call) => {
   989	            match &call.callee {
   990	                Expression::StaticMemberExpression(member) => match member.property.name.as_str() {
   991	                    "resolve" => {
   992	                        match &member.object {
   993	                            Expression::Identifier(ident) if ident.name.as_str() == "require" => {
   994	                                // `require.resolve("foo")`
   995	                            }
   996	                            Expression::MetaProperty(_) => {
   997	                                // `import.meta.resolve("foo")`
   998	                            }
   999	                            _ => return false,
  1000	                        }

I see! When the arrow function is formatted as a grouped last argument, it's reformatted with `FormatGroupedLastArgument` which passes `call_argument_layout: Some(GroupedCallArgumentLayout::GroupedLastArgument)`. This causes the arrow function to be formatted again with the `call_argument_layout` set.

Now in the `format` method, the `call_argument_layout` is used at lines 188-191:

```rust

    self.options.call_argument_layout,
    Some(GroupedCallArgumentLayout::GroupedLastArgument)
);
```

And then at line 212:
```rust
is_last_call_arg.then_some(&FormatTrailingCommas::All),
```

And at line 213:
```rust

```

But this is in the else branch (when `body_has_soft_line_break` is false). If `body_has_soft_line_break` is true, this code is not reached.

So for the printWidth 100 case, `body_has_soft_line_break` must still be `false`. Let me check why.

Wait, maybe my change didn't work because `body.span().start` doesn't point to the expression start. Let me add some debugging.

Actually, let me check if the comment is attached to the body or to the expression. The body `FunctionBody` contains one `ExpressionStatement`. The expression `context.visit(node)` has a leading comment `/** @type {Node} */`. The `body.span().start` should be the start of the first statement, which is the `ExpressionStatement`. The `ExpressionStatement` starts at the same position as the expression (since there's no leading keyword).

So `body.span().start` should point to `context` in `context.visit(node)`. The comment `/** @type {Node} */` is before `context.visit(node)`. So `f.comments().has_comment_before(body.span().start)` should return `true`.

But wait, maybe in the reformatted path (through `FormatGroupedLastArgument`), the comments have already been printed? Let me check: `FormatGroupedLastArgument` calls `FormatJsArrowFunctionExpression::new_with_options` which calls `format` which computes `body_has_soft_line_break`. At this point, the comments might already have been printed (since the arrow function was pre-formatted earlier). If the comments are already printed, `has_comment_before` might return `false`.

Yes! That's the issue. The first time the arrow function is formatted (pre-formatting in `write_grouped_arguments`), the comments are printed. The second time (through `FormatGroupedLastArgument`), the comments are already marked as printed, so `has_comment_before` returns `false`.

So my change only affects the first formatting, but the final formatting uses the reformatted version where the comments are already printed.

I need to also check for leading comments in the cached/printed state. But actually, the issue is that the arrow function is reformatted after pre-formatting. The pre-formatting determines if the grouped argument breaks, and then the final formatting is done again.

Let me look at the flow more carefully:

1. Pre-format all arguments (lines 649-714). For the arrow function (grouped last argument), it uses `FormatJsArrowFunctionExpression` with `cache_mode: Cache`. This computes `body_has_soft_line_break` with my change → `true` (since comments are still unprinted). The body is formatted with `[space(), format_body]`. The pre-formatted element does NOT break.

2. Since `grouped_breaks` is false, the code proceeds to create the grouped layout.

3. At line 800-807, the grouped element is reformatted with `FormatGroupedLastArgument`, which calls `FormatJsArrowFunctionExpression` again with `call_argument_layout: Some(GroupedCallArgumentLayout::GroupedLastArgument)`. This time, the comments have already been printed (from step 1), so `has_comment_before` returns `false`. Then `body_has_soft_line_break` is `false` (since the expression is a `CallExpression` without template/html embed). So it uses the else branch with `soft_line_indent_or_space`, which can break after `=>`.

That's the problem! The second formatting doesn't see the comments anymore.

So I need a different approach. Instead of checking for comments in `body_has_soft_line_break` (which is fragile because comments may already be printed), I should check something that persists.

Options:
1. Check if the body expression has comments in its span (not just before it). This would check if there are any comments within the body span, which would include the JSDoc comment.
2. Modify `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` to return `false` when the body has leading comments.
3. Cache the `body_has_soft_line_break` decision somehow.

Let me think about option 2: if we make the arrow function NOT groupable when it has leading comments on its body, then the call arguments would use the default layout (soft_block_indent), which would expand the arguments. The arrow function itself would still use `soft_line_indent_or_space` for its body, but since the call arguments are expanded, the arrow function would likely fit on its own line.

But wait, for printWidth 100, the second example currently breaks after `=>` because the arrow function is grouped. If we make it not groupable, the call arguments would expand, and the arrow function would be on its own line. That would match Prettier's behavior!

But would the arrow function still break after `=>` if it's on its own line? With `soft_line_indent_or_space`, if the arrow function fits on one line (which it does: `(node) => /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node))` is only ~55 chars), it would stay on one line. So the output would be:

```
const body = transform_body(
  state.analysis.instance_body,
  b.id("$.run"),
  (node) => /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node))
);
```

That's exactly what we want!

But wait, for printWidth 80, the first example currently outputs:
```
const result = items.map((child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child)));
```

This is because the arrow function is grouped and fits on one line. If we make it not groupable, the output might be:
```
const result = items.map(
  (child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))
);
```

But that's actually the Prettier output! The PR description says:
```
const result = items.map(
	(child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))
);
```

So making the arrow function not groupable when it has leading comments might be the right fix.

But wait, the input already has the call broken:
```js
const result = items.map(
  (child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))
);
```

If the arrow function is not groupable, the call arguments would expand. But would the formatter collapse it first? Let me check...

Actually, the formatter doesn't preserve the original formatting. It reformats based on the rules. If the arrow function is not groupable, the call arguments would use the default `soft_block_indent` layout. The formatter would try to fit everything on one line first. If it doesn't fit (due to print width), it would expand.

For `items.map((child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child)))`:
- At printWidth 80: the line is about 70 chars, which fits. So it would stay on one line: `items.map((child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child)))`.
- At printWidth 100: also fits.

But the expected Prettier output for the first example is:
```
const result = items.map(
	(child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))
);
```

So Prettier ALWAYS breaks the call arguments, even if it would fit on one line. That's because the input had them broken, and Prettier preserves the break?

Actually, wait. Let me re-read the PR description. The input is:
```js
const result = items.map(
  (child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))
);
```

And the Prettier output is the same:
```js
const result = items.map(
	(child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))
);
```

But the Oxfmt output is:
```js
const result = items.map((child) =>
	/** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))
);
```

So Oxfmt collapses the call arguments (putting them on one line) and then breaks after `=>`. Prettier preserves the broken call arguments and keeps the arrow on one line.

But would Prettier also collapse the call arguments if they fit? The PR description doesn't say. It just shows that Prettier preserves the original formatting (which has broken call arguments).

Actually, looking at this more carefully, I think the key issue is that Oxfmt is TOO aggressive in grouping the arrow function. It tries to collapse everything, and when it doesn't fit, it breaks after `=>` instead of expanding the call arguments.

Let me focus on making the arrow function NOT groupable when its body has leading comments. This way, the default call argument layout will handle it.

Let me modify `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` to check for leading comments on the body expression:



🎬 ACTION
str_replace_editor str_replace /testbed/crates/oxc_formatter/src/print/call_like_expression/arguments.rs   --old_str 'fn can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument(
    arrow_function: &ArrowFunctionExpression,
    is_arrow_recursion: bool,
    f: &Formatter<'"'"'_, '"'"'_>,
) -> bool {
    let body = &arrow_function.body;
    let return_type_annotation = &arrow_function.return_type;

    // Handles cases like:
    //
    // app.get("/", (req, res): void => {
    //     res.send("Hello World!");
    // });
    //
    // export class Thing implements OtherThing {
    //   do: (type: Type) => Provider<Prop> = memoize(
    //     (type: ObjectType): Provider<Opts> => {}
    //   );
    // }
    let can_group_type = return_type_annotation.as_ref().is_none_or(|any_type| {
        match &any_type.type_annotation {
            TSType::TSTypeReference(_) => {
                if arrow_function.expression {
                    return false;
                }
                body.statements.iter().any(|statement| match statement {
                    #[expect(clippy::match_same_arms)]
                    Statement::EmptyStatement(_) => {
                        // When the body contains an empty statement, comments in
                        // the body will get attached to that statement rather than
                        // the body itself, so they need to be checked for comments
                        // as well to ensure that the body is still considered
                        // groupable when those empty statements are removed by the
                        // printer.
                        // TODO: it seems no difference if we comment out this line
                        // comments.has_comments(s.span)
                        true
                    }
                    _ => true,
                }) || (body.statements.is_empty() && f.comments().has_comment_before(body.span.end))
            }
            _ => true,
        }
    });

    if !can_group_type {
        return false;
    }

    arrow_function.get_expression().is_none_or(|expr| match expr {
        Expression::ObjectExpression(_)
        | Expression::ArrayExpression(_)
        | Expression::JSXElement(_)
        | Expression::JSXFragment(_) => true,
        Expression::ArrowFunctionExpression(inner_arrow_function) => {
            can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument(inner_arrow_function, true, f)
        }
        Expression::ChainExpression(chain) => {
            matches!(chain.expression, ChainElement::CallExpression(_)) && !is_arrow_recursion
        }
        Expression::CallExpression(_) | Expression::ConditionalExpression(_) => !is_arrow_recursion,
        _ => false,
    })
}' --new_str 'fn can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument(
    arrow_function: &ArrowFunctionExpression,
    is_arrow_recursion: bool,
    f: &Formatter<'"'"'_, '"'"'_>,
) -> bool {
    let body = &arrow_function.body;
    let return_type_annotation = &arrow_function.return_type;

    // When the body has a leading comment (like a JSDoc inline type cast),
    // the arrow function should not be grouped. This matches Prettier'"'"'s behavior
    // for patterns like `(child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))`.
    // If grouped, the arrow would break after `=>` instead of expanding the call arguments.
    if f.comments().has_comment_before(body.span.start) {
        return false;
    }

    // Handles cases like:
    //
    // app.get("/", (req, res): void => {
    //     res.send("Hello World!");
    // });
    //
    // export class Thing implements OtherThing {
    //   do: (type: Type) => Provider<Prop> = memoize(
    //     (type: ObjectType): Provider<Opts> => {}
    //   );
    // }
    let can_group_type = return_type_annotation.as_ref().is_none_or(|any_type| {
        match &any_type.type_annotation {
            TSType::TSTypeReference(_) => {
                if arrow_function.expression {
                    return false;
                }
                body.statements.iter().any(|statement| match statement {
                    #[expect(clippy::match_same_arms)]
                    Statement::EmptyStatement(_) => {
                        // When the body contains an empty statement, comments in
                        // the body will get attached to that statement rather than
                        // the body itself, so they need to be checked for comments
                        // as well to ensure that the body is still considered
                        // groupable when those empty statements are removed by the
                        // printer.
                        // TODO: it seems no difference if we comment out this line
                        // comments.has_comments(s.span)
                        true
                    }
                    _ => true,
                }) || (body.statements.is_empty() && f.comments().has_comment_before(body.span.end))
            }
            _ => true,
        }
    });

    if !can_group_type {
        return false;
    }

    arrow_function.get_expression().is_none_or(|expr| match expr {
        Expression::ObjectExpression(_)
        | Expression::ArrayExpression(_)
        | Expression::JSXElement(_)
        | Expression::JSXFragment(_) => true,
        Expression::ArrowFunctionExpression(inner_arrow_function) => {
            can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument(inner_arrow_function, true, f)
        }
        Expression::ChainExpression(chain) => {
            matches!(chain.expression, ChainElement::CallExpression(_)) && !is_arrow_recursion
        }
        Expression::CallExpression(_) | Expression::ConditionalExpression(_) => !is_arrow_recursion,
        _ => false,
    })
}'

2026-05-31 11:55:42,469 - INFO - swea-agent-oxc-project__oxc-20857 - 💭 THOUGHT
Now both printWidth 80 and 100 produce the correct Prettier-like output:

For printWidth 80:
```
const result = items.map((child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child)));
const body = transform_body(
  state.analysis.instance_body,
  b.id("$.run"),
  (node) => /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node)),
);
```

For printWidth 100:
```
const result = items.map((child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child)));
const body = transform_body(
  state.analysis.instance_body,
  b.id("$.run"),
  (node) => /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node)),
);
```

Both now break the call arguments and keep the arrow function on one line! 

But wait, the first example `items.map((child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child)))` stays on one line for both widths. The PR description shows Prettier breaking the call arguments even for this case:

```js
const result = items.map(
	(child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))
);
```

But this might be because the input had the call arguments broken, and Prettier preserves that break. Or maybe Prettier also breaks because of the JSDoc comment length.

Let me check: at printWidth 80, `items.map((child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child)))` is about 70 chars, which fits. So Oxfmt keeps it on one line. That's acceptable behavior - if it fits, it's fine to keep it on one line. The PR's main complaint is about breaking after `=>` instead of the call arguments when it doesn't fit.

Now let me run the full test suite to see if my change breaks anything:

2026-05-31 12:00:57,901 - INFO - swea-agent-oxc-project__oxc-20857 - 💭 THOUGHT
I see the issue. My change is too broad: it prevents grouping of ANY arrow function that has leading comments on its body, not just JSDoc type casts.

Looking at the `arrow.js` test:
- `() /**/ => // () /**/ => /**/ () /**/ => /**/ { // }` - these arrows have leading comments (like `/**/`) on their body. My change now prevents them from being grouped, which changes the formatting.

And `last-argument-group.js`:
- `call(editor /* comment */, () => { // });` - This is a call with two arguments: `editor /* comment */` and `() => { // }`. The arrow function's body has a line comment `//`. My change now prevents grouping because the body has leading comments.

But wait, my change checks `f.comments().has_comment_before(body.span.start)`. For `() => { // }`, the body is a block statement with `{ // }`. The comment `//` is inside the block, not before the body. So `has_comment_before(body.span.start)` should return `false` because the comment is after the `{`.

But the test shows it's failing for the arrow chain case:
```
() /**/ => //
  () /**/ => /**/
    () /**/ => /**/ {
      //
    }
```

This is a chain of arrow functions. The first arrow `() /**/ => //` has a body that is another arrow function. The comment `/**/` is before the `=>` (trailing comment of params), and `//` is before the body. 

Actually, let me look at the failing diff more carefully:

Old output for printWidth 80:
```
call(() /**/ =>
  //
  /**/
  () /**/ => /**/ {
  //
});
```

New output:
```
call(
  () /**/ =>
    //
    () /**/ =>
    /**/
    () /**/ => /**/ {
      //
    },
);
```

So the call arguments are now expanded (broken out) instead of the arrow chain being grouped.

The issue is that my check `f.comments().has_comment_before(body.span.start)` returns `true` for the first arrow in the chain because its body (which is the next arrow function) has leading comments (the `//` and `/**/` comments).

Actually, the first arrow is `() /**/ => //` followed by another arrow. The body of the first arrow is the second arrow function. The comments `//` and `/**/` are before the second arrow function, which is the body of the first arrow. So `has_comment_before(body.span.start)` would be `true`.

But this is not a JSDoc inline type cast! It's just regular comments that happen to be before the body.

My change is too broad. I need to be more specific: only prevent grouping when the body has a leading JSDoc type cast comment (like `/** @type {X} */`), not just any comment.

Let me refine the check to only look for JSDoc type cast comments:

str_replace_editor str_replace /testbed/crates/oxc_formatter/src/print/call_like_expression/arguments.rs   --old_str 'fn can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument(
    arrow_function: &ArrowFunctionExpression,
    is_arrow_recursion: bool,
    f: &Formatter<'"'"'_, '"'"'_>,
) -> bool {
    let body = &arrow_function.body;
    let return_type_annotation = &arrow_function.return_type;

    // When the body has a leading comment (like a JSDoc inline type cast),
    // the arrow function should not be grouped. This matches Prettier'"'"'s behavior
    // for patterns like `(child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))`.
    // If grouped, the arrow would break after `=>` instead of expanding the call arguments.
    if f.comments().has_comment_before(body.span.start) {
        return false;
    }

    // Handles cases like:' --new_str 'fn can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument(
    arrow_function: &ArrowFunctionExpression,
    is_arrow_recursion: bool,
    f: &Formatter<'"'"'_, '"'"'_>,
) -> bool {
    let body = &arrow_function.body;
    let return_type_annotation = &arrow_function.return_type;

    // When the body expression has a leading JSDoc inline type cast comment
    // (like `/** @type {SomeType} */`), the arrow function should not be grouped.
    // This matches Prettier'"'"'s behavior for patterns like
    // `(child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))`.
    // If grouped, the arrow would break after `=>` instead of expanding the call arguments.
    if arrow_function.expression {
        if let Some(expr) = arrow_function.get_expression() {
            let expr_span = expr.span();
            if f.comments().has_comment_before(expr_span.start) {
                // Check if any leading comment is a JSDoc type cast comment (@type or @satisfies)
                let leading_comments = f.comments().comments_before(expr_span.start);
                if leading_comments.iter().any(|c| f.comments().is_type_cast_comment(c)) {
                    return false;
                }
            }
        }
    }

Wait, there's still a borrow issue. `comments_before_iter` returns an iterator that borrows `self` (the `Comments`). And then `is_type_cast_comment` also borrows `self`. But the iterator holds an immutable borrow, and `is_type_cast_comment` also needs an immutable borrow. In Rust, multiple immutable borrows are allowed! So this should be fine.

But actually, `comments_before_iter` is defined as:
```rust
pub fn comments_before_iter(&self, pos: u32) -> impl Iterator<Item = &Comment> {
```

This returns an iterator that borrows `self`. The `any` closure tries to call `f.comments().is_type_cast_comment(c)`. This calls `f.comments()` again, which returns a new reference to the same `Comments`. Since the first borrow (from the iterator) is still active, but both are immutable, this should work.

However, there might be a subtlety: `f.comments()` returns `&Comments<'_>`, and the iterator borrows from this reference. The second call `f.comments()` also returns `&Comments<'_>`. These are two separate immutable references to the same object, which is fine in Rust.

Let me try to compile and see if it works:

Only my new test fails now (the other two pass). The output looks correct:

For both printWidth 80 and 100:
```
const result = items.map((child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child)));

const body = transform_body(
  state.analysis.instance_body,
  b.id("$.run"),
  (node) => /** @type {Node} */ (context.visit(node)),
);
```

This matches the expected Prettier behavior! The call arguments are expanded (broken out) and the arrow function stays on one line.

But wait, the first example `items.map(...)` stays on one line even at printWidth 80. The PR description shows Prettier breaking it:
```
const result = items.map(
	(child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))
);
```

But this might be because the input already has the break, and Prettier preserves it. Or maybe Prettier breaks at printWidth 80 because the line is too long? Let me count: `const result = items.map((child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child)));` is about 75 characters. That fits in 80. So Prettier might preserve the original formatting.

Actually, looking at the snapshot, the output is correct for the second example. The first example fits on one line, which is acceptable. The PR's main issue is about the second example (and the general pattern), which now works correctly.

Now let me think about edge cases:

1. What if the JSDoc type cast is not the only leading comment? E.g., `(child) => // some comment\n/** @type {X} */ (visit(child))`. In this case, the first comment `// some comment` is a line comment that would already prevent grouping (because there's a comment before the body). Actually, my check only looks for type cast comments. If there's a non-type-cast comment before the body, grouping is still allowed. But that's the same as before my change.

2. What if the arrow function has a block body (not expression)? My check only applies when `arrow_function.expression` is true, which is correct because JSDoc inline type casts only appear in expression bodies.

3. What about `@satisfies` comments? The `is_type_cast_comment` function checks for both `@type` and `@satisfies`, so both are covered.

4. What about the case where the JSDoc type cast is inside a chain of arrow functions? This would be rare, but the check would apply to the innermost arrow that has the type cast.

Let me also verify that the PR examples work correctly. Let me create a more comprehensive test

- `const body = transform_body(...)` - Call arguments expanded, arrow on one line ✓
- `const a = arr.map((x) => /** @type {number} */ (x + 1));` - Stays on one line ✓
- `const b = arr.map((x) => /** @type {Foo} */ (process(x)));` - Stays on one line ✓
- `const c = arr.map((x) => /* something */ x + 1);` - Stays on one line (non-JSDoc, still grouped) ✓
- `const d = arr.map((x) => ...)` - Call arguments expanded, arrow breaks after `=>` (own-line JSDoc, not inline type cast) - this is correct because the comment is on its own line

Wait, for `const d`, the arrow still breaks after `=>`. But this is an own-line JSDoc comment, not an inline type cast. The expected behavior for this case would be that the arrow breaks after `=>` because the comment is on its own line. That matches the current behavior.

But looking at my test input for `const d`:
```js
const d = arr.map((x) =>
  /** @type {Foo} */
  (x + 1)
);
```

The output is:
```
const d = arr.map(
  (x) =>
    /** @type {Foo} */
    (x + 1),
);
```

This looks correct: the call arguments are expanded, and the arrow breaks after `=>` with the comment on the next line.

Now, there's one issue I notice: for `const a`, the input is `x => /** @type {number} */ (x + 1)` (without parens around `x`), but the output adds parens: `(x) => /** @type {number} */ (x + 1)`. This is due to the `arrowParens` option (always add parens). But that's not related to my change.

Now let me also check the non-JSDoc case `const c`: the input is `(x) => /* something */ (x + 1)`. The output keeps it on one line: `(x) => /* something */ x + 1`. Wait, it removed the parentheses around `x + 1`! That's because `ParenthesizedExpression` is not preserved. But more importantly, the arrow function is still grouped (the call stays on one line), which is correct because it's not a JSDoc type cast.

But wait, there's another issue: for `const c`, the non-JSDoc comment `/* something */` is still a leading comment on the body expression. My earlier change (reverted) would have affected this case. But my current change only checks for JSDoc type cast comments, so it correctly leaves this case alone.

Now I'm satisfied that the fix works correctly. Let me clean up the test file and accept the snapshots.

2026-05-31 12:14:28,907 - INFO - swea-agent-oxc-project__oxc-20857 - 💭 THOUGHT
All 261 tests pass (including my new test). 

Now let me think about edge cases and make sure the fix is complete:

1. **What about `@satisfies` JSDoc comments?** The `is_type_cast_comment` function already checks for both `@type` and `@satisfies`, so this is covered.

2. **What if the JSDoc type cast is in a non-call context?** E.g., `const x = (child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child));`. In this case, the arrow function is not a call argument, so `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument` is not called. The arrow function formatting uses the normal `body_has_soft_line_break` logic, which still uses `soft_line_indent_or_space`. This should be fine because there's no grouping to worry about.

3. **What about arrow functions in `.map()` with JSDoc type casts that fit on one line?** E.g., `items.map((x) => /** @type {number} */ (x + 1))`. If it fits on one line, it stays on one line (as shown in the test output). That's correct.

4. **What about when there are multiple JSDoc comments before the body?** The `any` check will find any type cast comment and prevent grouping.

5. **What about TypeScript `as` expressions vs JSDoc type casts?** The PR is specifically about JSDoc type casts. TypeScript `as` expressions like `(child) => (visit(child) as SomeType)` are handled by the existing `can_group_expression_argument` which checks for `TSAsExpression`. But there's already handling for that at line 558-559:
```rust
Expression::TSAsExpression(as_expression) => {
    can_group_expression_argument(&as_expression.expression, f)
}
```
And in `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument`, `TSAsExpression` is not in the match, so it would fall through to `_ => false`, meaning arrow functions with `as` expression bodies are not groupable. That's correct because `as` expressions have their own formatting needs.

Wait, actually `TSAsExpression` is not listed in the match in `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument`. Let me check: the match is on the body expression of the arrow function. If the body is `visit(child) as SomeType`, then the expression is a `TSAsExpression`. But `TSAsExpression` is not in the match arms, so it falls to `_ => false`. So arrows with `as` expressions are not groupable anyway.

But what about `(child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))`? The body expression is `visit(child)` (a `CallExpression`), and the comment is separate. So my check catches this.

I think the fix is complete and correct.

Let me also double-check that the change doesn't affect the `can_group_expression_argument` for the arrow function itself. The check I added is inside `can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument`, which is called from `can_group_expression_argument` when the argument is an `ArrowFunctionExpression`. So it correctly prevents grouping only for arrow functions with JSDoc type cast comments.

Let me also verify that the fix works for the first example in the PR description at printWidth 80 (where the line would be too long if not broken). The first example `items.map((child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child)))` is about 70 chars, which fits in 80. So it stays on one line. But if there were more arguments or longer names, it would break the call arguments. Let me test:







🎬 ACTION

const result = veryLongFunctionName(
  (child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))
);

2026-05-31 12:19:24,772 - INFO - swea-agent-oxc-project__oxc-20857 - Found submission: diff --git a/crates/oxc_formatter/src/print/call_like_expression/arguments.rs b/crates/oxc_formatter/src/print/call_like_expression/arguments.rs
index f33ea30..edfbc0f 100644
--- a/crates/oxc_formatter/src/print/call_like_expression/arguments.rs
+++ b/crates/oxc_formatter/src/print/call_like_expression/arguments.rs
@@ -577,6 +577,25 @@ fn can_group_arrow_function_expression_argument(
     let body = &arrow_function.body;
     let return_type_annotation = &arrow_function.return_type;
 
+    // When the body expression has a leading JSDoc inline type cast comment
+    // (like `/** @type {SomeType} */`), the arrow function should not be grouped.
+    // This matches Prettier's behavior for patterns like
+    // `(child) => /** @type {SomeType} */ (visit(child))`.
+    // If grouped, the arrow would break after `=>` instead of expanding the call arguments.
+    if arrow_function.expression {
+        if let Some(expr) = arrow_function.get_expression() {
+            let expr_span = expr.span();
+            // Collect whether any leading comment is a JSDoc type cast comment
+            let has_type_cast_comment = f
+                .comments()
+                .comments_before_iter(expr_span.start)
+                .any(|c| f.comments().is_type_cast_comment(c));
+            if has_type_cast_comment {
+                return false;
+            }
+        }
+    }
+
     // Handles cases like:
     //
     // app.get("/", (req, res): void => {
